# Prosecco.dev — AI Standards Directory

> A curated, open directory of AI agent interoperability standards, protocols, and specifications.
> Maintained by the community to help developers, researchers, and organizations navigate the evolving AI standards landscape.

## About

Prosecco.dev tracks emerging and approved standards for AI agent communication,
tool use, identity, and interoperability. Each standard is categorized by maturity
status (Backlog → Emerging → Draft → Approved), tagged by topic area, and linked
to its primary specification, community channels, and reference implementations.

## Site Map

- Homepage: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/
- Standards Radar (visual overview): https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/radar
- Timeline (activity over time): https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/timeline
- Affiliations (organizations & authors): https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/affiliations
- llms.txt: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/llms.txt
- llms-full.txt: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/llms-full.txt

## Approved Standards

### Agent Manifest — Core Declarative Specification v1.0 (AMCS/v1.0)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/593a86c1-6a78-4776-8452-daa379314658
- Organization: Agent Manifest Project
- Specification: https://agent-manifest-spec.org/spec/v1.0/agent_manifest_v1.0.html
- Tags: autonomous agents, governance, manifest, alignment, transparency, JSON-Schema
- Status: Approved
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A machine-readable, execution-agnostic standard that establishes the minimum declaration requirements for autonomous systems. It defines mandatory structural elements such as identity, purpose, autonomy levels, and stopping authority that must be present before an agent's execution.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Hernán Alfredo Capucci (Independent Research) — Author/Researcher

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification](https://agent-manifest-spec.org/spec/v1.0/agent_manifest_v1.0.html) (Primary Spec)

**What's New:**

The February 2026 release of **Version 1.0** marks the initial formalization of the Agent Manifest standard. Key highlights of this version include:

*   **Mandatory Structural Elements**: Requirements for identity, responsible party, and declared purpose.
*   **Autonomy & Control**: Formal definitions for "Autonomy Levels" and the requirement for a "Stopping Authority."
*   **Layered Architectural Model**: The introduction of the "Declaration Layer" as a distinct entity from enforcement and execution.
*   **Normative JSON Schema**: A technical foundation for developers to validate manifests for structural coherence.
*   **Negative Scope**: A new requirement for agents to explicitly declare operational boundaries and constraints.
*   **Execution-Agnostic Design**: The spec is designed to work across different AI architectures, from single agents to complex multi-agent workflows.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Agent Manifest — Core Declarative Specification v1.0** is a machine-readable, execution-agnostic standard designed to establish governance for autonomous systems. It introduces a formal **Declaration Layer** that requires software agents to declare their identity, purpose, and operational boundaries before they are permitted to interact with external systems or users. The core philosophy of the specification is that *legitimacy must precede execution*, addressing the growing asymmetry between rapid AI deployment and the lack of structured authority frameworks.

Key components of a conformant manifest include the identification of a **Responsible Party** (the accountable entity), a defined **Autonomy Level**, and a **Stopping Authority** (the mechanism or entity capable of terminating the agent's actions). The specification also covers "Negative Scope"—explicit declarations of what an agent is *not* permitted to do—alongside audit posture and data handling commitments.

Architecturally, the standard separates the ecosystem into three distinct layers:
1. **Declaration Layer**: Where the manifest resides (the focus of this spec).
2. **Enforcement Layer**: Policy engines or regulatory frameworks that evaluate the manifest.
3. **Execution Layer**: The runtime environment where the agent actually operates.

While the specification provides a normative JSON Schema to ensure structural validity and coherence, it explicitly does not define runtime behavior, enforcement mechanisms, or safety guarantees. It serves as a foundational "governance primitive" for the AI era.

---

### Agent2Agent Protocol (A2A)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/23435656-c711-4ee2-9bd1-01fcaeff89cd
- Organization: A2A Alliance
- Specification: https://a2a-protocol.org/
- Tags: Protocol, Agents
- Status: Approved
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

The Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol is a communication standard designed to enable seamless interoperability and interaction between autonomous AI agents. It provides a structured framework for agents to discover, negotiate, and collaborate on tasks across different platforms.

**Resources & Links:**

- [Documentation](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The Agent2Agent Protocol has recently moved into a major release phase with the announcement of **Version 1.0**. This milestone marks the shift from a specialized research project to a stable open standard hosted by **The Linux Foundation**. 

Recent updates include:
* **Stable Specification:** Transitioning to the 1.0 release candidate to provide a reliable foundation for enterprise AI agent integration.
* **Enhanced Documentation:** A new centralized documentation hub at `a2a-protocol.org` using the latest MkDocs Material framework to improve developer onboarding.
* **Governance Transition:** Formalizing the contribution process under Linux Foundation guidelines to encourage multi-vendor participation and vendor-neutrality.
* **Security Hardening:** Updated protocols for secure handshakes and encrypted messaging between autonomous agents.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol is an open standard developed under the auspices of The Linux Foundation, designed to enable secure communication, collaboration, and collective problem-solving between disparate AI agents. As AI systems become more specialized, the A2A protocol provides the necessary interoperability layer to allow these agents to discover one another, negotiate capabilities, and execute complex workflows across different platforms and organizational boundaries.

The protocol focuses on three core pillars: security, interoperability, and autonomy. It ensures that interactions are encrypted and authenticated, allowing agents to maintain high levels of trust while sharing data or sub-tasks. By establishing a common language and transport mechanism, A2A aims to prevent vendor lock-in and foster an ecosystem where a specialized "weather agent" can seamlessly provide data to a "logistics agent" without manual integration.

Key architectural components typically include discovery mechanisms, message schemas for task delegation, and settlement protocols for cross-agent resource sharing. The project is managed as an open-source initiative, encouraging contributions from AI researchers, enterprise software developers, and cloud service providers to ensure the standard evolves with the rapidly changing landscape of generative AI and autonomous systems.

---

### AGENTS.md (AGENTS.md)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/5a9be330-b282-49a0-9269-9f9888240a19
- Organization: Factory
- Specification: https://agents.md/
- Tags: Agents
- Status: Approved
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

AGENTS.md is a simple, open companion to README.md that gives coding agents a predictable, machine-readable place for project guidance (setup commands, test workflows, code style, and PR conventions) reducing README noise. Adopted by 20k+ GitHub repos and portable across tools like Cursor, Aider, RooCode, Zed, and more.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Matan Grinberg (Factory) — Contributor [profile](https://github.com/matangrinberg)
- Scott Wu (Cognition) — Contributor [profile](https://github.com/swu-cognition)
- Jules White (Google) — Contributor [profile](https://github.com/juleswhite)
- Romain Huet (OpenAI) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/romainhuet)
- Dylan Joss (augmentcode) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/dwjoss)
- Radu Mocanu — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/radu-mocanu)
- Harald Kirschner (Microsoft) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/digitarald)
- Andrew Kean Gao — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/andrewgcodes)
- sumama khan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sumamakhan761)
- colby-oai — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/colby-oai)
- Yunfan Yang — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kevinyang372)
- Tim Rogers (github) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/timrogers)
- Sly Gryphon (Telstra Purple; Gryphon Technology) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sgryphon)
- Siddhant Khare (gitpod-io) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Siddhant-K-code)
- Peter Dave Hello (DNSCrypt @nodejs @maintainers  @campus-experts  @transmission-remote-gui) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PeterDaveHello)
- Irina Chichikova (JetBrains) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/preigile)

- Abhijay Jain — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Abhijay007)

- Alice Hau — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ahau-square)
- BestCodes (BestCodes) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/The-Best-Codes)
- jh-block — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jh-block)
- Will Pfleger — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wpfleger96)
- Amed Rodriguez (Block) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/amed-xyz)
- Jarrod Sibbison — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jsibbison-square)
- Lam Chau — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lamchau)
- Angela Ning — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/angelahning)
- Nahiyan Khan (Block) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nahiyankhan)
- dorien-koelemeijer — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/dorien-koelemeijer)

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/agentsmd/agents.md) (Github)
- [Supported Agents Documentation (Aider)](https://aider.chat/docs/usage/conventions.html#always-load-conventions) (Documentation)
- [Supported Agents Documentation (Goose)](https://github.com/block/goose) (Github)
- [Supported Agents Documentation (Zed)](https://zed.dev/docs/ai/rules) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The **AGENTS.md** standard has recently gained significant momentum with formal adoption by major AI coding tools. **Zed** now explicitly lists `AGENTS.md` alongside `.cursorrules` and `CLAUDE.md` in its "Rules" documentation, allowing the editor's AI to automatically ingest project conventions. **Aider** has strengthened its documentation around "always load" conventions, recommending `AGENTS.md` for team-wide consistency. Meanwhile, **Goose**, the open-source agent from Block (formerly Square), has integrated the standard to facilitate autonomous task completion without manual context injection. The community is currently focusing on defining a structured schema within the Markdown format to help agents prioritize different types of instructions (e.g., separating "architecture" from "vulnerabilities").

**Discussion Summary:**

**AGENTS.md** is an emerging community standard designed to provide persistent, project-specific instructions and coding conventions to AI coding agents. Similar to established files like `CONTRIBUTING.md` for humans, `AGENTS.md` acts as a "handbook" for AI, ensuring that different tools—ranging from CLI-based agents like Aider and Goose to integrated IDEs like Zed—adhere to the same architectural patterns, style guides, and workflow requirements.

The primary goal of the standard is to solve the fragmentation of AI configuration files. Currently, various tools use proprietary files (e.g., `.cursorrules` for Cursor or `CLAUDE.md` for Claude Dev). `AGENTS.md` proposes a vendor-neutral alternative that can be committed to a git repository, allowing any supporting AI agent to gain immediate context about the project's tech stack, testing strategies, and preferred libraries.

Technically, the file is written in Markdown, making it easily readable by both LLMs and human developers. It typically includes sections for environment setup, build commands, linting rules, and specific "dos and don'ts" for code generation. By centralizing these instructions, teams can ensure consistent AI behavior across different development environments and avoid repetitive prompting. Adoption is growing among modern AI-native editors and tools that prioritize interoperability and "bring-your-own-context" workflows.

---

### Model Context Protocol (MCP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/e8d35071-696b-417f-b61f-f91bda91a32a
- Organization: Linux Foundation
- Specification: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/
- Tags: Protocol
- Status: Approved
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open protocol that enables seamless integration between LLM applications and external data sources and tools. Whether you're building an AI-powered IDE, enhancing a chat interface, or creating custom AI workflows, MCP provides a standardized way to connect LLMs with the context they need.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Anthropic (Anthropic) — Author

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol) (Github)
- [Official Blog](https://blog.modelcontextprotocol.io) (Blog)
- [Specification (2025-11-25)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25) (Documentation)
- [MCP Extensions Overview](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/extensions/overview) (Documentation)
- [Model Context Protocol Registry](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/registry/about) (Other)
- [Model Context Protocol Enhancements (SEPs)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/seps) (Working Group)

**What's New:**

The Model Context Protocol has recently introduced **MCP Extensions**, a framework designed to support domain-specific conventions and custom UI elements without altering the baseline protocol. This ensures core stability while enabling specialization in fields like finance or healthcare. Additionally, the community is actively processing **five independent Specification Enhancement Proposals (SEPs)** related to **Tool Annotations**. These proposals aim to establish a "Risk Vocabulary" that identifies whether a tool's action is read-only, idempotent, or potentially destructive. The project has also finalized its transition to a formal governance model under LF Projects, LLC, moving beyond its roots as a local utility into a production-standard for agentic AI workflows.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** is an open-standard communication protocol designed to provide a universal interface for integrating AI models with external data sources and tools. By creating a standardized way for "clients" (like AI-powered IDEs or chat interfaces) to interact with "servers" (which host specific datasets or local tools), MCP reduces the need for custom integrations.

The protocol has evolved from its initial focus on local tool wiring into a robust production-ready standard supported by a formal governance process under **LF Projects, LLC**. Key components of the specification include **Resources** (data provided to the model), **Tools** (executable functions), and **Prompts** (pre-defined templates). A significant recent architectural shift has introduced **MCP Extensions**, which allow developers to layer domain-specific conventions (e.g., healthcare or finance) and custom UI/authentication flows on top of the core protocol without destabilizing the base standard. 

Governance is managed through **Working Groups** and a formal **Specification Enhancement Proposal (SEP)** process. Current discussions within the community are heavily focused on "Risk Vocabulary," using tool annotations to help models and users understand the potential impact—such as destructive actions or external environment reach—before execution occurs.

---

## Draft Standards

### A Decoupled Authorization Model for Agent2Agent (A2A)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/100ae251-1af5-4270-a292-c249cabff4d3
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-chen-agent-decoupled-authorization-model/00/
- Tags: AI Agents, Authorization, Agent2Agent, Security, Just-in-Time Permissions
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This document proposes a framework for dynamic, intent-based authorization for AI Agents. It enables fine-grained, Just-in-Time (JIT) permissions based on an agent's specific intent and behavioral trustworthiness rather than long-lived identities.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Meiling Chen (China Mobile) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/chenmeiling90@hotmail.com)
- Li Su (China Mobile) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/suli@chinamobile.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)
- Emilio A. (Z1) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/emiliosanchez)
- Emilio Jiménez (www.yaco.es) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ejimenez)
- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)
- Paul Selkirk — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pselkirk)
- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)
- Sangho Na — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/microamp)
- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)

- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-chen-agent-decoupled-authorization-model/history/) (Other)
- [Text Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-chen-agent-decoupled-authorization-model-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-chen-agent-decoupled-authorization-model-00.html) (Documentation)
- [BibXML](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/bibxml3/draft-chen-agent-decoupled-authorization-model-00.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The standard is a newly introduced **Internet-Draft (version -00)** as of February 2026. The most significant recent activity is the formalization of the problem statement regarding AI Agent security, specifically identifying "Scale and Complexity Explosion" and "Identity Lifecycle Mismatch" as critical gaps in current IETF standards like OAuth 2.0. The authors from China Mobile have established the architectural split between the **Authorization Execution Point (AEP)** and the **Authorization Decision Point (ADP)**. Current work is focused on defining the "Input Contract," which will be the standardized data format used between these two points to enable intent-based decisions.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **A Decoupled Authorization Model for Agent2Agent** proposal introduces a security framework specifically designed for the unique lifecycle and operational patterns of AI Agents. Traditional authorization models (like RBAC) often rely on long-lived identities and static roles, which the authors argue are ill-suited for AI agents that are frequently ephemeral, autonomous, and multi-step in their task execution.

The core of the standard is the separation of authorization logic from business logic. It defines two primary logical components:
1.  **Authorization Execution Point (AEP):** A gateway that intercepts agent requests, validates foundational credentials (like OAuth 2.0 tokens), and assembles a context-rich query for a decision.
2.  **Authorization Decision Point (ADP):** The "brain" of the system that evaluates the AEP's query against declarative policies to return a real-time "Allow" or "Deny" decision.

Key innovations include **Intent-Based Authorization**, where permissions are granted based on the specific goal the agent is attempting to achieve (e.g., a specific flight query) rather than broad administrative scopes. It also addresses the need for **Just-in-Time (JIT) permissions** and **Continuous Trust Attestation**, moving away from "one-time" authentication at the start of a session. The document specifically analyzes OAuth 2.0, noting that while it provides a strong foundation for delegation, it lacks the fine-grained policy language and context-specific decision-making required for complex AI agent ecosystems. Currently, the specification is in its initial "00" draft phase, with several sections like the "Input Contract" and "Security Considerations" marked for future development (TBD).

---

### AAuth - Agentic Authorization OAuth 2.1 Extension (AAuth)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/2746ec5a-6edc-478a-bd67-d33c7737eca3
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-patwhite-aauth-00.html
- Tags: OAuth 2.1, AI Agents, Authorization, Agentic AI, Security
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An OAuth 2.1 extension defining the Agent Authorization Grant for confidential AI agents to request end-user consent and obtain access tokens via polling, SSE, or WebSockets. It introduces natural-language descriptions and a 'reason' parameter specifically for agentic workflows.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Pat White (IETF) — Author

**Resources & Links:**

- [Datatracker](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-patwhite-aauth/) (Documentation)
- [XML Source](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-patwhite-aauth-00.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The initial draft (**draft-patwhite-aauth-00**) was recently submitted to the IETF. It introduces the `agent_authorization` grant type, which allows AI agents to request tokens using a `reason` parameter to explain their intent. A major technical addition in this draft is the specification of asynchronous notification channels for token delivery, specifically defining how **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** and **WebSockets** should be used to push tokens to agents to avoid the overhead of constant HTTP polling. It also defines a new discovery mechanism where Resource Servers publish human-readable scope descriptions at `/.well-known/aauth.json` to improve the transparency of the user consent process.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Agentic Authorization (AAuth)** extension for OAuth 2.1 introduces a specialized grant type designed for **confidential agent clients**—autonomous software or AI processes that possess long-lived identities. While heavily inspired by the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628), AAuth is optimized for the asynchronous nature of AI agents and the need for high-context human-in-the-loop consent.

Key features of the standard include:
- **Agent Authorization Grant**: A new grant type (`urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:agent_authorization`) specifically for agents with a stable `client_id`.
- **Asynchronous Token Delivery**: Beyond standard HTTP polling, the spec introduces first-class support for **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** and **WebSockets** to deliver access tokens to the agent immediately upon user approval.
- **Enhanced Consent Context**: Agents provide a human-readable `reason` parameter in their request. The Authorization Server (AS) then fetches natural-language `scope_descriptions` from the Resource Server (RS) via a `.well-known/aauth.json` endpoint. This ensures users receive a clear explanation of *why* an agent is requesting specific permissions before granting access.
- **Separated UI**: Unlike standard OAuth flows, the agent does not handle redirects or UI rendering; it initiates the request and passively awaits the token while the AS manages the user interaction (e.g., via a mobile app or chat interface).

The standard aims to solve the problem of "black box" agent requests by making authorization requests more descriptive and easier to handle in event-driven AI architectures.

---

### Agent Attachment Protocol (AAP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/2255d169-51cf-4c29-b1e1-7bf467ce7cf4
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-dunbar-agent-attachment-00
- Tags: autonomous agents, overlay network, agent attachment, identity validation, A2G, Agent Gateway, network protocol
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A network-centric mechanism that enables autonomous agents to securely attach to an overlay network infrastructure. It defines procedures for identity validation, capability exchange, and secure session establishment between agents and network edge nodes.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Linda Dunbar (Futurewei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/dunbar.ll@gmail.com)
- Kausik Majumdar (Oracle) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/kausik.majumdar@oracle.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)

- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)

- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-dunbar-agent-attachment-00) (Primary Spec)
- [Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-dunbar-agent-attachment-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [BibXML](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/bibxml3/draft-dunbar-agent-attachment-00.xml) (Other)
- [Document History/Changes Feed](https://datatracker.ietf.org/feed/document-changes/draft-dunbar-agent-attachment/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The protocol was introduced in **March 2026** with the release of `draft-dunbar-agent-attachment-00`. This initial version establishes the foundational architecture for **Agent Gateways**, which serve as messaging routers that operate on transport headers without inspecting application payloads. Recent highlights include the definition of three specific gateway discovery mechanisms: **Anycast-based discovery**, **DHCP-based bootstrapping**, and **DNS-based resolution** via a directory service. It also introduces the **Message Forwarding Table (MFT)** concept, which allows gateways to map AgentIDs to local interfaces or next-hop gateways, ensuring stable communication even as agents change physical network locations.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Agent Attachment Protocol (AAP)** is a proposed network-centric framework designed to facilitate secure, autonomous agent interaction within an overlay network. Developed as an Individual Internet-Draft at the IETF, the protocol aims to decouple agent application logic from physical network infrastructure. By introducing **Agent Gateways (GWs)** as domain boundary nodes, the protocol allows agents to maintain stable, location-independent identifiers (**AgentIDs**) while moving across different administrative domains or cloud environments.

The protocol's scope is strictly limited to the **Agent-to-Gateway (A2G)** attachment and access plane. It defines standardized procedures for discovery, mutual authentication, capability exchange, and session management. Once an agent is attached to a local gateway, the gateway manages a **Message Forwarding Table (MFT)** to route encrypted traffic to other agents via a network of interconnected gateways. This architecture abstracts the underlying IP topology, effectively creating a policy-controlled messaging substrate similar to an SD-WAN overlay but tailored for AI-driven autonomous entities. Key features include support for multi-homing, mobility, and various discovery mechanisms such as Anycast, DHCP, and DNS. Additionally, AAP remains agnostic to application-layer messaging formats like A2A or MCP, focusing solely on transport-level forwarding.

---

### Agent Auth (AAuth) (AAuth)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/13fc44a1-6e02-4adf-b710-ec6b243516e6
- Organization: Dick Hardt Consulting
- Specification: https://github.com/dickhardt/AAuth/blob/main/draft-hardt-aauth.html
- Tags: AI Agents, Authentication, Authorization, Security, Identity
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

AAuth (Agent Auth) is a protocol designed to enable secure authentication and authorization for autonomous AI agents. It provides a technical framework for agents to prove their identity and obtain permissions to act on behalf of users or organizations.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Dick Hardt (Dick Hardt Consulting) — Author [profile](https://github.com/dickhardt)

**Resources & Links:**

- [AAuth GitHub Repository](https://github.com/dickhardt/AAuth) (Github)
- [Specification HTML (Source)](https://github.com/dickhardt/AAuth/blob/main/draft-hardt-aauth.html) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The most recent activity in the AAuth repository focuses on refining the **Agent Information** and **Capability Discovery** sections of the specification. Recent commits have introduced more detailed examples of how an agent presents its identity to a Resource Server without requiring a browser redirect. There is also new language regarding the use of short-lived 'capability tokens' to mitigate risk in autonomous environments. The draft-hardt-aauth.html file was recently synchronized with the latest community feedback, clarifying the requirements for Agent Registries.

**Discussion Summary:**

Agent Auth (AAuth) is an experimental protocol designed to enable autonomous agents to authenticate and interact with web services on behalf of a user. Traditional authentication protocols like OAuth are often optimized for human-in-the-loop interactions (e.g., redirecting to a browser for login), which are inefficient or impossible for fully autonomous AI agents. AAuth addresses this by providing a programmatic framework for credential exchange and capability delegation.

The standard introduces several key components:
- **Agent:** The software entity acting on the user's behalf.
- **Resource Server (RS):** The API or service the agent wishes to access.
- **Authorization Server (AS):** The entity that validates the user's identity and issues tokens.
- **Agent Registry:** A directory where agents can be identified and their public keys or capabilities verified.

Key technical goals include reducing latency in agent-to-service communication, supporting non-interactive authentication flows, and maintaining high security through cryptographic proofs of agent identity. It leverages existing standards where possible (such as JWTs and DID/verifiable credentials) but optimizes the handshake for high-frequency, automated requests. AAuth is currently in the early drafting stage, primarily hosted on GitHub to solicit feedback from the developer and AI research communities.

---

### Agent Authorization Profile (AAP) for OAuth 2.0 (AAP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/01dd5ba2-8887-4957-a42e-4fa091afa4fd
- Organization: IETF Shattered (Individual)
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-aap-oauth-profile/
- Tags: OAuth 2.0, JWT, AI Agents, Authorization, Security, Machine-to-Machine
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An authorization profile for OAuth 2.0 and JWT designed for autonomous AI agents. It extends existing standards with structured claims for agent identity, task context, operational constraints, and human oversight.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- angel cruz (Individual) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/bullgram@gmail.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)

- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)

- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)

- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-aap-oauth-profile/history/) (Other)
- [Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-aap-oauth-profile-01.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Version Parent](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-aap-oauth-profile-01.html) (Documentation)
- [Draft Email Mailing List](mailto:draft-aap-oauth-profile@ietf.org) (Mailing List)

**What's New:**

The latest update (version -01) introduces several critical technical refinements to the Agent Authorization Profile. Key additions include a detailed **Action Name Grammar (ABNF)** for standardizing how capabilities are requested, and a comprehensive **JSON Schema** in the appendix for validating AAP tokens. The update significantly expands the **Security and Privacy Considerations** sections, offering specific guidance on data minimization, cross-domain correlation, and cryptographic key management. Furthermore, version -01 provides elaborated **Token Exchange** examples that demonstrate "Privilege Reduction," showing how an agent can exchange a broad token for a more restricted, tool-specific one while maintaining an auditable delegation chain. New error codes specifically for AAP-related failures (e.g., `insufficient_oversight`, `delegation_depth_exceeded`) have also been registered.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Agent Authorization Profile (AAP) for OAuth 2.0 is a specialized authorization profile designed specifically for autonomous AI agents. Built upon existing standards like OAuth 2.0 (RFC 6749) and JSON Web Tokens (JWT, RFC 7519), AAP addresses the unique security challenges posed by AI agents, such as high-frequency autonomous actions, the absence of real-time human approval, and the need for complex delegation across multiple tools. 

Key technical contributions of the profile include:
- **Structured Claims:** Introduction of new JWT claims to represent agent identity (`agt`), task context (`tsk`), and operational constraints (`cns`).
- **Capability-Based Authorization:** Moving beyond simple scopes to "capabilities" that include fine-grained action permissions and lifecycle constraints.
- **Delegation Management:** Defining an auditable delegation chain (`dlg`) that tracks the history of token exchanges and ensures privilege reduction at each level.
- **Human Oversight:** Mechanisms to encode requirements for human intervention or approval within the token itself.
- **Proof-of-Possession:** Strict requirements for binding tokens to the agent's cryptographic identity to prevent impersonation and token theft.

The profile is intended for Standards Track status and focuses on machine-to-machine (M2M) scenarios where context-aware, auditable authorization is critical. It explicitly avoids defining internal AI model behaviors or ethical decision-making, focusing instead on the security handshake between agents, Authorization Servers, and Resource Servers.

---

### Agent Client Protocol (ACP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/06f4f926-c3ca-4880-85a1-9d7dfa999f65
- Organization: Zed
- Specification: https://agentclientprotocol.com/
- Tags: Protocol, Agents
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) is a standardized communication protocol designed to connect AI agents with various client interfaces. It provides a common architecture for session management, initialization, and registry discovery between agents and clients.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Zed Industries (Zed) — Author

**Resources & Links:**

- [Architecture Documentation](https://agentclientprotocol.com/get-started/architecture) (Documentation)
- [ACP Registry](https://agentclientprotocol.com/get-started/registry) (Documentation)
- [Protocol Overview](https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/overview) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) has recently introduced its core **Architecture** and **Registry** frameworks. The most significant recent development is the establishment of a standardized **JSON-RPC** interface that allows clients (like the Zed editor) to communicate with external AI agents regardless of their underlying language (Python, TypeScript, etc.). Additionally, the **ACP Registry** is now live, providing a centralized hub where developers can register and discover agents. Recent documentation updates emphasize the "LSP for Agents" philosophy, focusing on providing a universal way for agents to describe their tools and capabilities to host applications. Implementation guides for building ACP-compliant agents are now the primary focus for the community developers.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) is an open standard designed to facilitate seamless communication between AI agents and the applications (clients) they interact with. Often compared to how the Language Server Protocol (LSP) revolutionized code editors, ACP aims to eliminate the need for custom integrations by providing a universal interface for agent-based interactions. The architecture is built on a client-server model where 'agents' act as servers that expose specific capabilities, and 'clients' (such as IDEs, chat interfaces, or CLI tools) consume these capabilities.

Key architectural components include a **JSON-RPC** based messaging system, ensuring lightweight and scalable communication. The protocol defines a standardized set of lifecycle events, capability discovery mechanisms, and execution flows. A significant part of the ecosystem is the **ACP Registry**, a centralized or federated directory that allows developers to discover, share, and install ACP-compatible agents easily. This registry functions similarly to package managers like npm or PyPI but is tailored for autonomous AI agents.

The protocol focuses on several key areas: 
- **Tool Discovery:** Allowing agents to broadcast their available functions and tools to the client.
- **Resource Management:** Managing the context and data access shared between the client and the agent.
- **Security and Permissions:** Ensuring that agent actions, especially those involving file system access or API calls, are performed within defined boundaries.

By standardizing these interactions, ACP enables developers to build an agent once and deploy it across any ACP-compliant host, significantly reducing the fragmentation in the burgeoning AI agent ecosystem.

---

### Agent Communication Protocol (ACP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/10ca6368-74ec-495c-9f68-aef5d14c050e
- Organization: IBM
- Specification: https://agentcommunicationprotocol.dev/
- Tags: Protocol, Agents
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

The Agent Communication Protocol (ACP) is a standardized messaging and interaction framework designed to enable interoperability between AI agents. It defines a consistent architecture and lifecycle for agent runs, complemented by SDKs for Python and TypeScript.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Bee Team (IBM) — Author
- Tomas Pilar (Apoco) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pilartomas)
- Matous Havlena (Apoco) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/matoushavlena)
- Tomáš Weiss — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/tomkis)
- Jenna Winkler — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jenna-winkler)
- Sandi Besen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sandijean90)
- Jan Pokorný (https://apoco.com/) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JanPokorny)
- Radek Ježek — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jezekra1)
- Vishal V — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/cptnm3)
- Ismael Faro Sertage (IBM Research) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ismaelfaro)
- Lukáš Janeček (Apoco) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/xjacka)
- deanchanter — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/deanchanter)
- Tomáš Dvořák — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Tomas2D)
- Pavel Bucka — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/penge)
- Mark Sturdevant — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/markstur)
- Jan Dušek — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Zycon42)
- Kai Wedekind (IBM Deutschland GmbH) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/KaiWedekind)
- Kate Blair (IBM) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/geneknit)
- Gabe Goodhart (IBM) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gabe-l-hart)
- Anirban Basu — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/anirbanbasu)
- Daniel Benner (IBM) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/dbennerIBM)
- Doc Jones (GraphQL Lead at Postman Open Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/doc-jones)
- E B Benson  — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Deriverx2)
- Emmanuel Ferdman — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/emmanuel-ferdman)
- Ian Molloy (IBM Research) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/imolloy)
- Milan Gallas — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/GALLLASMILAN)
- Paolo Dettori (IBM) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pdettori)
- Titus Lim — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/tituslhy)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Python SDK](https://github.com/i-am-bee/acp/tree/main/python) (Github)
- [TypeScript SDK](https://github.com/i-am-bee/acp/tree/main/typescript) (Github)
- [Documentation](https://agentcommunicationprotocol.dev/introduction/welcome) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The Agent Communication Protocol has recently seen the launch of its formal documentation site (`agentcommunicationprotocol.dev`), providing structured guides for developers. Recent activity in the GitHub repositories indicates a focus on stabilizing the **Python and TypeScript SDKs**. Key updates include improved error handling for inter-agent messaging and refined schema definitions for task hand-offs. The community is currently soliciting feedback on the 'Agent Discovery' phase of the protocol to ensure it scales across decentralized environments.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Agent Communication Protocol (ACP)** is an emerging open-source standard designed to enable seamless interaction and interoperability between disparate AI agents and agentic systems. Developed by the "i-am-bee" community, the protocol addresses the fragmentation in the AI agent ecosystem by providing a unified communication layer. It defines how agents should discover each other, exchange messages, share state, and hand off tasks.

Key technical components of the standard include:
- **Unified Message Schema**: A standardized format for requests, responses, and event-based signaling between agents.
- **Service Discovery**: Mechanisms that allow agents to identify the capabilities and endpoints of other agents within a network.
- **Multi-Language SDKs**: Implementation support is currently provided through official Python and TypeScript/JavaScript SDKs, facilitating integration into existing AI frameworks (like LangChain or Bee Agent Framework).
- **Transport Independence**: While primarily implemented over HTTP/WebSockets in initial drafts, the protocol is designed to be transport-agnostic to support various networking environments.

The protocol aims to move the industry toward "Agentic Interoperability," where a user can orchestrate a swarm of agents built on different platforms to solve complex tasks without manual glue-code. By standardizing the interface, ACP reduces the barrier for developers to build modular, composable agent ecosystems.

---

### Agent Directory Service (ADS)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/dd67ac4e-f746-4162-901f-95ab09be6fc7
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-mp-agntcy-ads/
- Tags: AI Agents, Directory Service, Discovery, Metadata, Content Routing, Internet-Draft
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

The Agent Directory Service (ADS) is a distributed directory service designed to store metadata for AI agent applications. It enables the discovery of agents with specific skills through directory records and an interconnected content-routing protocol.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Luca Muscariello (IEEE) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/muscariello@ieee.org)

- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)

- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)

- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Main Specification (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-mp-agntcy-ads-01.html) (Primary Spec)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-mp-agntcy-ads/history/) (Other)
- [Plain Text Specification](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-mp-agntcy-ads-01.txt) (Primary Spec)
- [XML Specification](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-mp-agntcy-ads-01.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The latest update (version **-01**, released February 2024) significantly refines the **Storage Architecture** and **Discovery Process**. Key additions include:

*   **OCI Integration:** Formalized the use of OCI Image Manifests for storing agent records as "Record Artifacts," ensuring compatibility with the broader container ecosystem.
*   **Two-Level Mapping Architecture:** Detailed the DHT-based discovery process, specifically how skills map to Content Identifiers (CIDs) and CIDs map to PeerIDs.
*   **Skill Taxonomy:** Introduced hierarchical skill taxonomies for search optimization, allowing for multi-dimensional searches across domains and modules.
*   **Security Model Enhancements:** Added comprehensive sections on cryptographic integrity, digital signatures for provenance, and threat mitigation strategies within decentralized networks.
*   **Open Agent Schema Framework (OASF):** Deepened the integration with OASF for structured metadata modeling.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Agent Directory Service (ADS) is a distributed directory infrastructure designed specifically for the AI agent ecosystem. It provides a standardized mechanism to store, discover, and verify metadata for AI agent applications (Multi-Agent Systems or MAS). The service addresses the complexities of agent discovery by allowing developers to search for agents based on specific skills, performance characteristics (cost, latency), and resource requirements. 

Architecturally, ADS is built on decentralized storage principles. it utilizes **Open Container Initiative (OCI)** specifications to package agent records as artifacts, allowing the standard to leverage existing container registry tools, security scanners, and authentication mechanisms. A core component of the system is the use of **Content Identifiers (CIDs)**, which ensure data immutability, deduplication, and location independence. 

For discovery, ADS employs a two-level mapping architecture using **Distributed Hash Tables (DHT)** and content-routing protocols. This enables efficient matching of agent capabilities against hierarchical skill taxonomies (using the **Open Agent Schema Framework - OASF**). The standard also emphasizes security through cryptographic integrity, digital signatures for content provenance, and trust boundaries to mitigate threats in decentralized networks. By using OASF, ADS can handle both objective metrics like GPU requirements and subjective evaluations like user ratings.

---

### Agent Identity Protocol: Agentic Authentication and Authorized Policy Enforcement (AIP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/1a4e443a-b956-408d-b90d-6b505ec3939b
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-aip-agent-identity-protocol/
- Tags: AI Agents, Identity, Authentication, Security, Policy Enforcement, Cryptography
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An open standard for verifiable identity and policy enforcement for AI agents, providing unique identifiers and cryptographic signing for agent actions. It utilizes a two-layer architecture comprising an identity registry and an enforcement proxy to ensure secure, authorized tool execution.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- James Cao (Montcao) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/james@montcao.com)
- Carlos Eduardo Arango Gutierrez (NVIDIA) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/eduardoa@nvidia.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)

- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)

- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)

- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)

- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)
- Sangho Na — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/microamp)
- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)

- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)

- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-aip-agent-identity-protocol-00.html) (Primary Spec)
- [Primary Specification (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-aip-agent-identity-protocol-00.txt) (Primary Spec)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-aip-agent-identity-protocol/history/) (Other)
- [BibXML](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/bibxml3/draft-aip-agent-identity-protocol-00.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The standard is currently in its inaugural phase with the release of **draft-aip-agent-identity-protocol-00** on March 16, 2026. Key newly defined components include the **AIP Token** for agent attestation, the specification for an **AIP Registry** to manage public keys, and the introduction of a YAML-based **AgentPolicy** schema. The protocol specifically introduces "Human-in-the-Loop" (HITL) and Data Loss Prevention (DLP) rules as core components of the enforcement layer. activity is currently focused on the initial solicitation of feedback from the IETF community.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Agent Identity Protocol (AIP) is an IETF Internet-Draft proposed to establish a standardized framework for the identity and authorization of AI agents. The protocol addresses the "identity gap" where AI agents currently operate using human credentials, making it impossible for downstream services to distinguish between human and automated actions. This lack of distinction leads to security vulnerabilities, auditability challenges, and compliance issues.

The AIP is architected into two distinct but cooperative layers:
*   **Layer 1 (Identity):** Establishes a verifiable identity for every agent. Agents are registered with an AIP Registry, which assigns a unique Agent ID and stores the agent's public key. Agents must then sign every outbound tool call with their private key, creating a "AIP Token" (Agent Attestation Token) for verification.
*   **Layer 2 (Enforcement):** Utilizes an AIP Proxy that sits between the AI client and tool servers. This proxy intercepts calls, verifies signatures against the registry, and evaluates requests against a declarative **AgentPolicy**. This policy can trigger outcomes such as allowing the call, denying it, or putting it on hold for "Human-in-the-Loop" (HITL) approval.

The protocol is designed to be transport-agnostic but identifies the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as its primary initial target for tool-call interfaces. It does not replace existing authentication methods like OAuth or mTLS but adds a necessary layer of agent-specific attribution and policy control.

---

### Agent Message Protocol (AMP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/b2067b9d-2d7b-435f-b89a-b80424911214
- Organization: AMP
- Specification: https://github.com/AMProtocol/AMP/blob/main/validator/spec/v0.3.md
- Tags: AI Agents, Data Economy, Protocol, Validator, Interoperability
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

AMP is an open protocol and registry designed for the AI agent data economy. It provides a specification for validators to ensure the integrity and interoperability of data exchanged between AI agents.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- AMProtocol Team (AMP) — Author
- BrandonWeber-Nymbl — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/BrandonWeber-Nymbl)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (v0.3)](https://github.com/AMProtocol/AMP/blob/main/validator/spec/v0.3.md) (Primary Spec)
- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/AMProtocol/AMP) (Github)

**What's New:**

The latest update, **version 0.3**, focuses heavily on the **Validator Specification**. This version introduces more rigorous schema validation for agent-to-agent messages, ensuring that all communications are cryptographically signed and formatted according to strict protocol rules. Significant improvements have been made to the `validator/spec` directory in the official repository, providing developers with a clearer roadmap for implementing validator nodes. Recent community activity has also centered on improving the project's documentation and establishing a more robust CI/CD pipeline for the protocol's reference implementation. Efforts are now shifting toward defining "Plugin" architectures that allow the protocol to be extended for specific industry use cases like DeFi and supply chain management.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Agent Message Protocol (AMP)** is an open standard designed to facilitate seamless communication and interoperability between autonomous AI agents and validator nodes. Developed by the AMProtocol community, it establishes a structured format for agents to exchange data, execute tasks, and verify actions within a decentralized or distributed environment. By providing a common interface, AMP aims to prevent ecosystem fragmentation and ensure that different agentic systems can collaborate regardless of their underlying architecture.

The core of the protocol (v0.3) defines how messages are structured, signed, and validated. Key components include a validator specification that ensures messages adhere to safety and business logic rules before being committed or acted upon. It addresses critical challenges in AI agent interactions, such as identity verification, message integrity, and standardized task handoffs. The protocol is currently in an active developmental phase, with the community focused on refining the validator logic and expanding the feature set for complex multi-agent workflows.

---

### Agent Name Service (ANS): A Universal Directory for Secure AI Agent Discovery and Interoperability (ANS)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/77c981ce-3ab4-481c-b7dc-c6696d977be1
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-narajala-ans-00
- Tags: AI Agents, Discovery, DNS, Interoperability, PKI, Identity
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Agent Name Service (ANS) is a DNS-inspired architecture and universal directory protocol designed for secure AI agent discovery. It provides a protocol-agnostic registry using PKI certificates to verify agent identity and supports multiple communication standards like A2A, MCP, and ACP.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Ken Huang (DistributedApps.ai) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/ken.huang@distributedapps.ai)
- Vineeth Sai Narajala (Amazon Web Services) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/abcvineeth.sai@gmail.com)
- Idan Habler (Intuit) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/idan_habler@intuit.com)
- Akram Sheriff (Cisco Systems) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/isheriff@cisco.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)

- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)

- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)

- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)

- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)

- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-narajala-ans-00) (Primary Spec)
- [Plain Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-narajala-ans-00.txt) (Other)
- [XML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-narajala-ans-00.xml) (Other)
- [BibTeX Reference](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-narajala-ans/00/bibtex/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The Agent Name Service (ANS) has been introduced via its first Internet-Draft (`draft-narajala-ans-00`) in May 2025. This initial release establishes a foundational architecture for AI agent discovery, featuring a modular **Protocol Adapter Layer** to support MCP, ACP, and A2A protocols. Key recent developments include the formalization of a DNS-like naming convention (e.g., `protocol://agentID.capability.provider.version`) and the integration of PKI-based lifecycle management. The draft also introduces a **MAESTRO-based threat analysis** to secure agent registries against impersonation and poisoning, and provides a structured JSON Schema for protocol-agnostic communication between agents and the directory service.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Agent Name Service (ANS) is a proposed universal directory architecture designed to address the critical lack of a public framework for discovering and verifying AI agents. As agent-to-agent (A2A) traffic is projected to become a major portion of internet data, ANS provides a protocol-agnostic registry that bridges emerging standards like Google's A2A, the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and the Agent Communication Protocol (ACP). 

The framework is heavily inspired by the Domain Name System (DNS) but enhances it with semantic and security features necessary for autonomous agents. At its core, ANS leverages Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) for verifiable identity, using X.509 digital certificates to establish a root of trust through Certificate Authorities (CA) and Registration Authorities (RA). This ensures that agent discovery isn't just about finding a network address, but about verifying the agent's identity and its specific capabilities.

Key architectural innovations include a **Protocol Adapter Layer**, which translates between the registry's internal data and various external protocol formats, and a **Formal Naming Structure** (e.g., `protocol://agentID.capability.provider.version`) that allows for granular, capability-aware resolution. The specification also introduces a structured request/response schema based on JSON Schema to handle lifecycle events like registration, renewal, and revocation. 

Security is a primary focus of the standard, featuring a comprehensive threat analysis based on the MAESTRO framework. It addresses risks such as agent impersonation, registry poisoning, and Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks through mandatory PKI integration and secure resolution algorithms. By providing this foundational infrastructure layer, ANS aims to enable a scalable, trustworthy, and interoperable ecosystem for the next generation of AI agents.

---

### Agent Operation Authorization (AOA)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/45894230-2568-4421-a287-69c6d0f06bcd
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-liu-agent-operation-authorization/02/
- Tags: AI Agents, Authorization, JWT, Security, Delegation, Identity
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A framework that enables verifiable delegation of actions from human principals to autonomous AI agents using structured JWTs. It provides a mechanism for fine-grained authorization of agent operations to prevent unauthorized or hallucinated actions through cryptographic verification.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Dapeng Liu (Alibaba) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/maxpassion@gmail.com)
- Judy Zhu (Alibaba) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/hongru.zhr@alibaba-inc.com)
- Suresh Krishnan (Cisco Systems) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/suresh.krishnan@gmail.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
