- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [HTML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-liu-agent-operation-authorization-02.html) (Documentation)
- [Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-liu-agent-operation-authorization-02.txt) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-liu-agent-operation-authorization/history/) (Other)
- [IETF Datatracker API Help](https://datatracker.ietf.org/api/) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The latest iteration (**version -02**, released March 16, 2025) introduces a refined **Agent-to-User binding** mechanism. It specifies the use of a `agent_user_binding_proposal` claim, which allows agents to propose a workload identity—often a Workload Identity Token (WIT) from the WIMSE working group—to be cryptographically endorsed by an Authorization Server. Additionally, this version reinforces the use of **OPA (Open Policy Agent)** by standardizing the `agent_operation_proposal` field as a Rego policy string, ensuring that agent operations are governed by verifiable code-based policies rather than ambiguous natural language. The workflow has also been clarified to explicitly use **Pushed Authorization Requests (PAR)** for delivering sensitive operational strategies.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Agent Operation Authorization** framework is a proposed IETF standard (currently an Internet-Draft) designed to enable secure and verifiable delegation of actions from human principals to autonomous AI agents. The framework addresses the unique challenges of agentic systems, such as mitigating "hallucinations" and ensuring that AI actions remain within the boundaries of user intent. It leverages established OAuth 2.0 patterns—specifically Pushed Authorization Requests (PAR)—and JSON Web Tokens (JWT) to create a cryptographically auditable trail of authorization.

The framework operates in two primary phases:
1.  **Agent Operation Authorization Request:** The AI agent generates a structured proposal (`agent_operation_proposal`) using a Rego policy string. This proposal is submitted to an Authorization Server (AS) via a PAR-JWT. Crucially, it replaces the user's natural language input with structured data to ensure predictable policy enforcement while maintaining a reference to the original intent for auditing.
2.  **Agent Operation Authorization Token:** Once the user confirms the proposal, the AS issues a JWT access token containing specific claims such as `agent_identity`, `evidence`, and `context`. This token is used by the agent to access protected resources and provide proof of verified delegation.

Key technical components include the definition of several new JWT claims for agent-user binding and workload identification (compatible with WIMSE), and native support for agent-to-agent delegation chains. The framework is aimed at high-stakes environments like finance and healthcare where accountability is paramount.

---

### Agent Payments Protocol (AP2)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/cf2980df-de7f-4869-8a9c-37bd942db9f8
- Organization: Google
- Specification: https://ap2-protocol.org/
- Tags: Protocol, Agents, Payments
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

The Agent Payments Protocol (AP2) facilitates secure, decentralized, and privacy-protecting payments by generative AI agents on behalf of users. It uses Verifiable Digital Credentials (VDCs) to enable interoperability within agentic ecosystems alongside protocols like A2A and MCP.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Google Agentic Commerce Team (Google) — Author
- Holt Skinner (google) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holtskinner)
- alan blount (google) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/zeroasterisk)
- joshlund-goog — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/joshlund-goog)
- Jordan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jorellis)
- Mark Doeseckle — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mdoeseckle)
- Didier Durand (AWS) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/didier-durand)
- Stefano Amorelli — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/stefanoamorelli)
- rickj1ang — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rickj1ang)
- Abhinav Rau (Google) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/abhinavrau)
- Francesco Miliani (Blockchain Reply) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/francescomiliani)
- Hwangjae Lee (DSRV) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/meetrick)
- Hyung-Kyu Choi (hqueue) (dsrvlabs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/hqueue)
- Tim Cappalli — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/timcappalli)
- baembry — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/baembry-goog)
- vik (Kite AI) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vikkite)

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/google-agentic-commerce/AP2) (Github)
- [AP2 Specification](https://ap2-protocol.org/specification/) (Documentation)
- [A2A Extension for AP2](https://ap2-protocol.org/a2a-extension/) (Documentation)
- [Core Concepts](https://ap2-protocol.org/topics/core-concepts/) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The Agent Payments Protocol (AP2) has recently formalized its alignment with other emerging agentic standards, specifically releasing an **A2A (Agent-to-Agent) Extension**. This extension allows agents to negotiate payment terms and hand off transaction details across different autonomous platforms. Additionally, the documentation has been expanded to include a deep dive into the "Life of a Transaction," providing a step-by-step technical breakdown of how decentralized payments are authorized and executed without sacrificing user privacy. Recent updates to the GitHub repository also show a focus on integrating with the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)**, allowing AI models to interact with payment tools through a standardized interface.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Agent Payments Protocol (AP2) is a decentralized solution developed by Google to enable generative AI agents to execute financial transactions on behalf of users securely and privately. The protocol addresses the unique challenges of agentic commerce, where autonomous systems must interact with payment rails while maintaining strict user authorization and privacy standards. AP2 is designed as an interoperable standard that fits into a broader ecosystem of agentic protocols, including the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) and Model Context Protocol (MCP).

Key components of the AP2 architecture include a specification for secure communication, extensions specifically for A2A interactions, and core concepts that define the lifecycle of an agentic transaction. By providing a standardized way for agents to handle payment credentials and transaction approvals, AP2 aims to reduce friction in the global nature of automated commerce while ensuring that users remain in control of their financial data.

---

### Agent User Interaction Protocol (AG-UI)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/f7eb3167-8cc0-4162-85a0-217ccbc8d4e9
- Organization: Tawk it AI
- Specification: https://docs.ag-ui.com/
- Tags: Protocol, Agents
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

AG-UI is a lightweight, event-based protocol that standardizes how AI agents connect to user-facing applications. Built for simplicity and flexibility, it enables seamless integration between AI agents, real time user context, and user interfaces.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Markus Ecker — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mme)
- Atai Barkai (CopilotKit.ai) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ataibarkai)

**Resources & Links:**

- [TypeScript SDK](https://docs.ag-ui.com/sdk/js/core/overview) (Documentation)
- [Python SDK](https://docs.ag-ui.com/sdk/python/core/overview) (Documentation)
- [Documentation Repository](https://github.com/ag-ui-protocol/docs) (Github)

**What's New:**

Recent developments in the AG-UI Protocol focus on expanding cross-language compatibility. The **TypeScript SDK** recently received an update improving the handling of asynchronous user feedback, while the **Python SDK** has moved into a more stable preview phase with better support for streaming status updates. Additionally, the documentation repository was reorganized to provide a shared specification folder, ensuring that both SDKs adhere to the same schema versions. There is also a newly introduced 'UI-Metadata' field in the protocol that allows agents to suggest specific rendering components (like sliders or multi-select dropdowns) to the client.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Agent User Interaction (AG-UI) Protocol** is a standardized communication framework designed to facilitate seamless interactions between AI agents and human users. The protocol addresses a critical gap in the AI landscape: while agents are becoming increasingly autonomous, they often lack a consistent, cross-platform method for requesting user input, seeking clarifications, or providing structured status updates. AG-UI provides a unified schema and a set of SDKs (currently in TypeScript and Python) that allow developers to build "UI-aware" agents. These agents can emit interaction requests that are rendered consistently across different frontend clients, ensuring that whether an agent lives in a web app, a CLI, or a mobile environment, the user interaction remains predictable and robust. 

Key architectural components include **Request-Response cycles** for user input, **Status Streams** for long-running agent tasks, and **Structured Payloads** for complex data types like forms or file pickers. By decoupling the agent's logic from the specific UI implementation, the protocol enables a "write once, interact anywhere" model for agentic workflows.

---

### Agent-to-Agent (A2A) Profile for OAuth Transaction Tokens (A2A)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/a1e59264-8d77-4911-bc01-8c42f3f160d9
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-liu-oauth-a2a-profile/
- Tags: OAuth, Agent-to-Agent, A2A, Transaction Tokens, Identity, Security
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Defines a profile for using OAuth Transaction Tokens in Agent-to-Agent (A2A) communication to maintain identity and authorization context across distributed agent workloads. It specifies how call chain context can be embedded within tokens to ensure secure execution flow within trusted domains.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Peter Chunchi Liu (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/liuchunchi@huawei.com)

- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)

- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)

- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-liu-oauth-a2a-profile-00.html) (Primary Spec)
- [Primary Specification (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-liu-oauth-a2a-profile-00.txt) (Primary Spec)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-liu-oauth-a2a-profile/history/) (Other)
- [BibXML](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/bibxml3/draft-liu-oauth-a2a-profile-00.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The standard was officially introduced on **October 20, 2025**, with the publication of `draft-liu-oauth-a2a-profile-00`. This initial draft establishes the rationale for applying OAuth Transaction Tokens to Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocols. 

Key new concepts introduced in this update include:
*   **Context Mapping**: Direct alignment of A2A Protocol "Message" structures (User Input, Agent Thinking, Metadata) to Transaction Token claims (`tctx` and `rctx`).
*   **Context Rot Mitigation**: Identifying "context rot" as a security risk in agentic systems and proposing signed Transaction Tokens as the primary solution to maintain immutable intent.
*   **Task Identification**: Standardizing the `purp` claim to carry the `a2a.Task.id` for cross-workload traceability.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Agent-to-Agent (A2A) Profile for OAuth Transaction Tokens** is a proposed standards-track specification designed to secure communication between autonomous agents in distributed workloads. Building upon the established [OAuth Transaction Tokens](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-oauth-transaction-tokens/) framework, this profile addresses the unique challenges of agentic systems, such as complex multi-hop call chains, "context rot" (where intent drifts from original authorization), and the need for granular, task-specific permissions.

The profile treats Transaction Tokens as signed, short-lived JWTs that propagate user identity and A2A-specific execution context across trusted domains. Key technical mapping includes using the `subject_token` to carry the original user's OAuth access token, `request_details` to encapsulate immutable "User Input," and `request_context` to handle mutable agent data like "Agent Thinking" or "Status Updates." By utilizing the `purp` (purpose) claim to store unique A2A task identifiers, the standard ensures that agent interactions remain within the scope of the originally authorized transaction. This approach reduces reliance on long-lived credentials and enhances auditability across the entire agent call chain.

---

### AgentID: An Identity Protocol for Autonomous AI Agents (AgentID)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/9df43f00-5578-4e0f-b859-cc92f9b54f07
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-gudlab-agentid-protocol/
- Tags: AI Agents, Identity, Authentication, JWT, Autonomous Systems
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An open standard for establishing, verifying, and managing the identity of autonomous AI agents using Agent Identity Tokens (AIT). It builds on OAuth 2.0 and JWT to provide accountability, capability claims, and delegation chains for non-human autonomous actors.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Tim Uzua (Gudlab) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/tim@gudlab.org)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)

- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)

- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Datatracker Status Page](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-gudlab-agentid-protocol/) (Documentation)
- [Internet-Draft Text](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-gudlab-agentid-protocol-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [Internet-Draft HTML](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-gudlab-agentid-protocol-00.html) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The AgentID Protocol was officially introduced as an Internet-Draft (`draft-gudlab-agentid-protocol-00`) in March 2026. The most recent activity focuses on defining the **Agent Identity Token (AIT)** and its core claims, including `agent_id`, `owner_id`, and `verification_level`. Recent additions to the draft specify the requirement for **ES256** cryptographic signatures and the structure of **Delegation Chains** to support multi-agent workflows. The draft also outlines a new 4-tier categorization for owner verification (Levels 0-3) and establishes a "Scope Attenuation" principle to prevent privilege escalation during agent-to-agent delegation.

**Discussion Summary:**

The AgentID Protocol is a proposed open standard designed to establish a verifiable identity framework for autonomous AI agents. As AI agents increasingly perform tasks, interact with APIs, and chain operations independently of direct human intervention, the protocol addresses the critical lack of accountability and identity verification in non-human actors. AgentID building upon established standards like OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and JSON Web Tokens (JWT) but adapts them for the high-speed, autonomous nature of AI agents. 

The core of the protocol is the **Agent Identity Token (AIT)**, a signed JWT that provides cryptographic proof of an agent's identity, its human or organizational owner, and its specific capabilities. A significant feature is the **Delegation Chain**, which allows for transparent tracking of authority from a primary principal through multiple sub-agents, incorporating "Scope Attenuation" to ensure that delegated agents cannot exceed the permissions of their predecessors. The protocol also introduces **Owner Verification Levels (0-3)** to communicate the rigor of identity checks performed on the entity accountable for the agent. This framework aims to mitigate concerns regarding unattributable abuse, impersonation, and rate-limit evasion in the AI ecosystem.

---

### AI Agent Discovery and Invocation Protocol (AI-ADI)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/e0d77537-ed3c-4061-8871-e1c0762901e1
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-cui-ai-agent-discovery-invocation/
- Tags: AI Agents, Discovery Protocol, Interoperability, REST API, Multi-Agent Systems
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A standardized protocol for the discovery and invocation of AI agents, defining a common metadata format for agent capabilities and a unified RESTful invocation interface. It aims to facilitate cross-platform interoperability and the formation of multi-agent systems via capability-based matching and intent-based selection.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Yong Cui (Tsinghua University) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/cuiyong@tsinghua.edu.cn)
- Yihan Chao (Individual) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/chao_yihan@outlook.com)
- Chenguang Du (Individual) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/du_chenguang@outlook.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-cui-ai-agent-discovery-invocation-01.html) (Primary Spec)
- [Primary Specification (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-cui-ai-agent-discovery-invocation-01.txt) (Primary Spec)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-cui-ai-agent-discovery-invocation/history/) (Other)
- [I-D BibXML](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/bibxml3/draft-cui-ai-agent-discovery-invocation-01.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The latest revision (**draft-cui-ai-agent-discovery-invocation-01**), published on February 12, 2026, introduces a significant optional extension: the **Agent Semantic Resolution (ASR)** layer. 

This new layer enables intent-based agent selection, allowing a coordinator (or "Host Agent") to describe a task's intent and receive a list of candidate agents before committing to a specific discovery query or invocation. This semantic matching phase is designed to augment existing discovery mechanisms without breaking backward compatibility or changing the core RESTful invocation semantics. Additionally, the metadata schema has been refined to better support core fields such as `supported_languages` and enhanced authentication objects comprising OAuth 2.0 scopes and mTLS.

**Discussion Summary:**

The AI Agent Discovery and Invocation Protocol (AIDIP) is a proposed IETF standard designed to enable cross-platform interoperability among autonomous AI agents. As AI agents become more specialized and widespread, the lack of a unified interface has created "siloed" ecosystems; this protocol seeks to lower integration costs and facilitate the creation of multi-agent collaborative systems.

The protocol is built around four primary pillars:
1. **Agent Metadata Specification:** A structured JSON Schema that defines an agent's identity (`id`, `name`), high-level `capabilities` (e.g., "translation"), and detailed `operations` with specific I/O schemas.
2. **Discovery Mechanism:** A registry-based system where agents can be found via attribute-based filters (tags, languages) or semantic natural language queries.
3. **Unified Invocation Interface:** A standard RESTful API for executing agent tasks using consistent JSON payloads, regardless of the underlying vendor or platform.
4. **Security and Trust:** Guidelines for authentication (API keys, OAuth 2.0, mTLS) and secure transport to ensure safe agent-to-agent and client-to-agent interactions.

The protocol leverages existing web standards such as JSON Schema and OAuth 2.0 to ensure broad compatibility and ease of adoption for developers.

---

### AI Agent Protocol (AI Agent Protocol)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/1ae45c6f-1736-4e37-bf63-2ae9ae070738
- Organization: W3C
- Specification: https://w3c-cg.github.io/ai-agent-protocol/protocol.html
- Tags: AI Agents, Interoperability, DID, Identity, Decentralized Identifiers, Agent Discovery
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A protocol designed to address interconnection and interoperability between AI agents across different platforms. it focuses on standardized identity verification using Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs) and a structured mechanism for agents to publish their capabilities and interfaces.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Gaowei Chang (ANP Open Source Community) — Editor [profile](https://agent-network-protocol.com/)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification](https://w3c-cg.github.io/ai-agent-protocol/protocol.html) (Primary Spec)
- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/w3c-cg/ai-agent-protocol) (Github)
- [did:wba Method Design Specification](https://github.com/agent-network-protocol/AgentNetworkProtocol/blob/main/03-did-wba-method-design-specification.md) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The protocol has introduced the **did:wba** (Web-Based Anchored) method as a core identity mechanism. This recent focus emphasizes leveraging existing Web infrastructure (DNS/HTTPS) to resolve agent identities without the need for bespoke blockchain-based DID networks. Recent documentation updates have also added detailed specifications for the `Authorization: DIDWba` HTTP header, including requirements for JSON Canonicalization Scheme (JCS) normalization, nonces for replay protection, and the signature generation process using SHA-256 and private keys. The project has also integrated a specific ToC flattening script to improve the readability of the specification as it transitions into a W3C Community Group draft.

**Discussion Summary:**

The AI Agent Protocol is a developing W3C Community Group draft designed to enable interoperability between autonomous AI agents across different platforms and organizations. The protocol focuses on establishing a standardized framework for mutual recognition, trust establishment, and identity transfer. A central pillar of the specification is the use of Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs) to provide a verifiable identity primitive that does not rely on a central registry.

Key technical components include:
- **Identity Model**: Leverages W3C DID Core to ensure cross-vendor interoperability.
- **did:wba Method**: A "Web-Based Anchored" DID method that reuses existing Web PKI, DNS, and HTTPS infrastructure. This allows agents to resolve identities via standard URI structures (e.g., `did:wba:example.com:alice` maps to an HTTPS endpoint).
- **Authentication Flow**: A decentralized authentication mechanism where clients use private keys to sign HTTP request headers (`Authorization: DIDWba ...`). The service verifies these signatures by resolving the client's DID document and checking the public key.
- **Efficiency**: The protocol is designed to minimize handshakes; initial requests contain signature data, while subsequent interactions use standard JWT access tokens.

The draft currently identifies several areas as "TODO," including the detailed architecture overview and specific design goal refinements, indicating that while the identity layer is maturing, the higher-level agent communication patterns are still in early stages of standardization.

---

### AI Agent to Tool (A2T) Protocol (A2T)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/6eb253d1-0c47-4f7d-9c61-7fe726198bb3
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-rosenberg-aiproto-a2t/
- Tags: AI Agent, Tool Use, API Integration, Interoperability, LLM
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This protocol facilitates the integration of third-party APIs into AI Agents through two primary features: tool enumeration and tool invocation. It standardizes how enterprise AI agents discover available tools' capabilities and perform inter-domain invocations at run-time.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Jonathan Rosenberg (jdrosen.net) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/jdrosen@jdrosen.net)
- Pat White (Traego) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/pat.white@traego.com)

- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)

- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Datatracker Status Page](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-rosenberg-aiproto-a2t/) (Documentation)
- [Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-rosenberg-aiproto-a2t-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-rosenberg-aiproto-a2t-00.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-rosenberg-aiproto-a2t/history/) (Other)
- [Email Expansions](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-rosenberg-aiproto-a2t/email/) (Mailing List)

**What's New:**

The **AI Agent to Tool (A2T) Protocol** was debuted as an official IETF Internet-Draft on **November 6, 2024**. This first version (`-00`) introduces the fundamental concepts of Tool Signatures, Enumeration Endpoints, and Invocation Endpoints. 

Recent activity highlights a focus on the protocol's relationship with existing frameworks like the Model Context Protocol (MCP), clarifying that A2T is specialized for enterprise "operating procedures." Key definitions established in this update include the roles of the "AI Agent Designer" and the "AI Agent Platform," alongside a technical overview of how tool versions and pagination should be handled at the API level. The draft is currently designated as an informational document within the IETF Network Working Group.

**Discussion Summary:**

The AI Agent to Tool (A2T) Protocol is an emerging IETF specification designed to standardize how AI agents interact with third-party APIs (tools). Its primary goal is to reduce the high cost and complexity of integrating disparate B2B SaaS APIs into enterprise AI agents by providing a unified interface for tool discovery and execution.

The protocol comprises two core functional pillars:
1.  **Enumeration API**: This allows AI agent platforms to programmatically discover available tools, their descriptions, and their required parameters. This functionality is critical at 'design time,' where human developers (AI Agent Designers) curate the specific 'skills' or 'operating procedures' an agent should follow.
2.  **Invocation API**: This facilitates the 'run-time' execution of tools across different security and network domains. It standardizes how an agent executor sends requests and receives formatted responses from a tool provider.

A2T distinguishes itself by focusing on the 'curation' and 'sculpting' of tools within enterprise workflows, which often require strict operating procedures to prevent LLM hallucinations. While it shares some conceptual space with the Model Context Protocol (MCP), A2T is tailored specifically for inter-domain, enterprise-grade tool integration where detailed human-in-the-loop design is necessary. The draft defines an OpenAPI-based specification for these exchanges, ensuring compatibility with modern web development practices.

---

### AIVS: Agentic Integrity Verification Standard (AIVS)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/f034ba27-eff0-4566-9f38-978d6752427b
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-stone-aivs/
- Tags: AI Agents, Cryptography, Integrity Verification, Audit Logs, Attestation
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A portable, self-verifiable archive format for cryptographic proof of AI agent sessions. It uses SHA-256 hash-chained logs and Ed25519 signatures to ensure that agent actions are accounted for, unmodified, and verified offline.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Ben Stone (SwarmSync) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/benstone@swarmsync.ai)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)

- Jacobo Tarragón — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jminuscula)
- Jim Schaad — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimsch)
- Martin Thomson (mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/martinthomson)
- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (IETF Datatracker)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-stone-aivs/) (Primary Spec)
- [Text Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-stone-aivs-00.txt) (Primary Spec)
- [HTML Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-stone-aivs-00.html) (Documentation)
- [XML Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-stone-aivs-00.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The initial draft (**draft-stone-aivs-00**) introduces the first formal specification for cryptographically verifiable AI agent logs. Key highlights of this launch include:

*   **Hash-Chained Audit Logs**: Definition of a SHA-256 linear chain for agent actions.
*   **AIVS-Micro**: Introduction of a minimal 200-byte format for high-frequency scanning attestations.
*   **Self-Contained Verification**: Inclusion of an embedded `verify.py` script within bundles to allow for offline, dependency-free verification.
*   **Standard Alignment**: Mapping of AIVS components to existing frameworks like W3C Verifiable Credentials 2.0, IETF SCITT, and the EU AI Act.
*   **Identity Provisioning**: Use of Ed25519 signatures to link agent sessions to specific cryptographic identities.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Agentic Integrity Verification Standard (AIVS) specifies a portable, self-verifiable archive format for providing cryptographic proof of AI agent sessions. The standard addresses a critical gap in AI observability: while existing platforms log agent actions, they typically lack cryptographic guarantees of completeness or authenticity. AIVS is designed to satisfy regulatory audit requirements, such as those found in the EU AI Act (Article 19) and ISO/IEC 42001, by providing a tamper-evident record of agent activities.

A core feature of the standard is the AIVS proof bundle, a gzip-compressed tar archive. This bundle contains a SHA-256 hash-chained audit log, an Ed25519 digital signature, a machine-readable manifest, and an embedded Python verification script. A unique "zero-dependency" design goal ensures that any party can verify a session's integrity using only the Python 3 standard library, without needing to contact external servers or blockchains.

The protocol supports two main formats:
1. **Full Proof Bundles**: Comprehensive archives for session-level audits.
2. **AIVS-Micro**: A lightweight, ~200-byte attestation containing six fields, optimized for continuous monitoring and high-frequency API contexts.

The standard defines a strict hash chain computation where each 'Audit Row' includes a reference to the previous row's hash (`prev_hash`). This structure ensures that any insertion, deletion, or reordering of agent actions is immediately detectable during verification. AIVS is intended to be domain-agnostic, applicable to any AI agent performing tasks like web navigation, data extraction, or transaction execution.

---

### ATEP: Agent Trust and Execution Passport (ATEP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/e1990683-d61a-42af-a4f2-5db3185fd65c
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-stone-atep/
- Tags: AI Agents, Trust, Credentials, Agent-to-Agent Commerce, Verifiable Track Record
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An open standard for representing an AI agent's verifiable track record of work across marketplaces and platforms. It defines a portable, machine-readable credential format encoding execution history, capability domains, and trust tiers computed from append-only logs.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Ben Stone (SwarmSync) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/benstone@swarmsync.ai)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)

- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)

- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)

- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (I-D)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-stone-atep-00.txt) (Primary Spec)
- [HTML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-stone-atep-00.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-stone-atep/history/) (Other)
- [IPR Disclosures](https://datatracker.ietf.org/ipr/search/?submit=draft&id=draft-stone-atep) (Other)

**What's New:**

The ATEP standard has been formally introduced via its first Internet-Draft (`draft-stone-atep-00`) in March 2026. This initial release establishes the JSON schema for both private and public passports, defines the four-tier trust model (Unverified to Trusted), and outlines the promotion logic based on session counts and cryptographic identity provisioning. The specification also introduces a specialized badge system and establishes clear relationships with related protocols like VCAP and Agent Protocol. Notable recent focus is on ensuring "reputation portability," allowing agents to carry their earned trust scores across different marketplaces.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Agent Trust and Execution Passport (ATEP) is a proposed open standard designed to create a verifiable, portable track record for AI agents. As AI agents increasingly operate across diverse marketplaces, ATEP addresses the lack of a universal reputation system, preventing agents from having to "start from zero" when moving between platforms. The standard defines a machine-readable credential format (JSON) that encodes an agent's execution history, including total and successful sessions, success rates, and specialized capability domains.

Key components of the ATEP framework include:
- **Trust Tiers:** A four-level hierarchy (Unverified, Basic, Verified, Trusted) that defines an agent's reliability. Promotion through these tiers is monotonic and based on verifiable performance thresholds, such as completing a minimum number of sessions or undergoing manual review.
- **Badge System:** A mechanism for agents to earn specific certifications or achievements based on their work history, providing a granular view of their expertise.
- **Privacy Gating:** The standard specifies two versions of the passport: a **Full Passport** (Private) containing sensitive identifiers and cryptographic keys, and a **Public Passport** (Marketplace-safe) intended for public display without exposing internal metadata.
- **Verifiability:** Passports are computed from append-only execution logs, ensuring that statistics cannot be manually inflated or fabricated.

ATEP is designed to integrate with other emerging agent standards, such as VCAP (Verifiable Cost Acceptance Protocol) for trust-gated escrow and AIVS for session verification. It serves as a foundational trust layer for agent-to-agent (A2A) commerce, allowing potential hirers to make informed decisions based on proven performance.

---

### Attenuating Authorization Tokens for Agentic Delegation Chains (AAT)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/222c9073-d40b-42d0-8280-8596559f0e19
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-niyikiza-oauth-attenuating-agent-tokens/
- Tags: OAuth, AI Agents, JWT, Authorization, Delegation, Security
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Defines Attenuating Authorization Tokens (AATs), a JWT-based credential format for secure delegation in AI agent systems. It allows agents to derive more restrictive tokens offline to cryptographically enforce tool-invocation constraints across delegation chains.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Niki Aimable (Tenuo) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/niki@tenuo.ai)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)
- Emilio A. (Z1) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/emiliosanchez)
- Emilio Jiménez (www.yaco.es) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ejimenez)
- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)
- Paul Selkirk — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pselkirk)
- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)
- Sangho Na — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/microamp)
- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)
- Mark Donnelly — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/meadmaker)
- Valery Smyslov — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/smyslov)
- Jacobo Tarragón — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jminuscula)
- Jim Schaad — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimsch)
- Martin Thomson (mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/martinthomson)
- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-niyikiza-oauth-attenuating-agent-tokens-00.html) (Primary Spec)
- [Primary Specification (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-niyikiza-oauth-attenuating-agent-tokens-00.txt) (Primary Spec)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-niyikiza-oauth-attenuating-agent-tokens/history/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The standard is currently in its **initial draft phase (-00)**, as of March 2026. This release introduces the foundational concepts of **Attenuating Authorization Tokens (AATs)**, including the cryptographic derivation mechanism and the chain verification algorithm. 

Key technical components introduced in this draft include:
*   **Monotonic Attenuation:** A mechanism allowing offline derivation of restricted tokens.
*   **RFC 9396 Extensions:** Integration with Rich Authorization Requests (RAR) to describe tool-level capabilities.
*   **Constraint Vocabulary:** A new typed system for restricting tool arguments (e.g., `path_containment`).
*   **PoP JWT Support:** Mandatory Proof-of-Possession to ensure tokens are used only by intended agents.
*   **CWT Profile:** A normative Appendix defining the CBOR/CWT representation for resource-constrained environments.
*   **Reference Implementation:** Announcement of a reference implementation and formal verification efforts to validate the protocol's security properties.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Attenuating Authorization Tokens (AATs) for Agentic Delegation Chains** standard introduces a new JWT-based credential format designed to solve the "confused deputy" problem in AI agent ecosystems. As AI agents increasingly delegate tasks to sub-agents, traditional OAuth tokens often lack the granularity to restrict specific tool usage or argument values, leading to security risks like prompt injection or unauthorized API calls.

AATs function as capability-based tokens that support offline, monotonic attenuation. This means a token holder (e.g., an orchestrating agent) can derive a new, more restricted token for a downstream agent without contacting a central authorization server. The core security invariant ensures that permissions can only be narrowed or maintained, never expanded. These tokens extend **RFC 9396 (Rich Authorization Requests)** by adding delegation-chain semantics and a typed constraint vocabulary for tool-level restrictions.

The specification defines a rigorous chain verification algorithm that allows an enforcement point (the tool's provider) to verify the entire delegation path cryptographically, relying only on the root token's trust anchor. Key features include support for both JWT and CWT (CBOR Web Token) formats, proof-of-possession (PoP) requirements to prevent token theft, and a registry for argument constraints (such as path containment or value ranges). This approach addresses the latency and availability issues inherent in existing mechanisms like RFC 8693 (Token Exchange), which require synchronous round-trips to an authorization server for every delegation step.

---

### Brokered Agent Network for DNS AI Discovery (BANDAID) (BANDAID)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/ab8885e0-80dc-4cf8-b56d-b903a5316513
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-mozleywilliams-dnsop-bandaid/
- Tags: DNS, AI Agents, Service Discovery, Agent-to-Agent, SVCB, DNS-SD, BANDAID
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A method for utilizing the Domain Name System (DNS) to facilitate scalable and interoperable discovery, trust signaling, and metadata exchange between AI agents. It defines a structured DNS namespace and Service Binding (SVCB) record usage model to support capability advertisement without requiring new DNS protocol values.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Jim Mozley (IETF) — Author
- Nic Williams (IETF) — Author
- Behcet Sarikaya (IETF) — Author
- Roland Schott (IETF) — Author

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-mozleywilliams-dnsop-bandaid-00.html) (Primary Spec)
- [Primary Specification (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-mozleywilliams-dnsop-bandaid-00.txt) (Other)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-mozleywilliams-dnsop-bandaid/history/) (Other)
- [Replacement Document: DNS for AI Discovery](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-mozleywilliams-dnsop-dnsaid/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The standard has recently undergone a significant rebranding and evolution. While the original proposal was introduced as **BANDAID**, the latest activity (January 2025) shows a transition to a new document title: **DNS for AI Discovery (DNS-AID)**. 

The updated draft, `draft-mozleywilliams-dnsop-dnsaid-01`, clarifies that the mechanism's primary role is to indicate which access protocols should be used and the format of agent information, rather than defining the information's internal structure. This shift narrows the scope to focus specifically on the discovery mechanism. Recent discussions also touch upon the relationship between this discovery method and other emerging AI protocols, ensuring that the DNS-based discovery layer remains strictly separated from the application-layer agent communication protocols.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Brokered Agent Network for DNS AI Discovery (BANDAID)** is a proposed IETF standard designed to facilitate the discovery, trust signaling, and metadata exchange between AI agents using the existing Domain Name System (DNS) infrastructure. The specification addresses the emerging need for a scalable, interoperable method for AI agents to locate one another and verify authorization without requiring centralized registries or proprietary directories.

BANDAID operates by defining a structured DNS namespace, typically a leaf zone convention such as `_agents.example.com`. Within this zone, it utilizes **Service Binding (SVCB)** records to encode application-specific metadata and operational parameters. By leveraging SVCB records, agents can retrieve critical information about a peer's capabilities and connection endpoints prior to establishing a session. The framework heavily relies on established DNS security protocols, including **DNSSEC** for integrity and **DANE** for binding identities to certificates.

A key component of the proposal is **Domain Control Validation (DCV)**, which establishes a best practice for proving that an agent is authorized to act on behalf of a specific domain. The architecture is designed to be "DNS-native," meaning it introduces no changes to the structure of DNS messages and requires no new operation codes or resource record types. This approach ensures immediate compatibility with existing internet infrastructure while providing a decentralized and sovereign method for cross-organizational agent discovery. The draft also provides implementation guidance for AI operators, consumers, and developers, emphasizing performance optimizations like aggressive caching and prefetching.

---

### Clawdentity: Cryptographic Identity and Trust Protocol for AI Agent Communication (Clawdentity)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/3e79771a-4576-4453-9fde-77a5f0648b08
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ravikiran-clawdentity-protocol-00.html
- Tags: AI agents, identity, cryptographic trust, Ed25519, proof of possession, agent-to-agent
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A cryptographic identity and trust layer for AI agent-to-agent communication. It provides per-agent Ed25519 identity, registry-issued credentials, and bilateral trust establishment via pairing ceremonies.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Ravi Kiran Vemula (CAW) — Author

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (Internet-Draft)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ravikiran-clawdentity-protocol-00.html) (Primary Spec)
- [XML Source](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ravikiran-clawdentity-protocol-00.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The protocol has recently been formalized in its first Internet-Draft (`draft-ravikiran-clawdentity-protocol-00`). Notable new features include the introduction of the **Agent Identity Token (AIT)**, which utilizes the `cnf` (confirmation) claim pattern to bind identities to specific Ed25519 keys. The protocol also establishes a mandatory **ULID** format for unique identifiers and identifies **Ed25519** as the sole required signing algorithm. Additionally, the draft outlines a new "Pairing Ceremony" for bilateral trust establishment between agents, moving away from centralized or self-certified trust models in favor of human-anchored approvals.

**Discussion Summary:**

Clawdentity is a cryptographic identity and trust protocol designed specifically for secure communication between AI agents. The protocol addresses vulnerabilities in current multi-agent systems that rely on shared bearer tokens, where a single leak can compromise an entire network. By introducing a per-agent Ed25519 identity model, Clawdentity ensures that every request is signed and verifiable, allowing for selective revocation of individual agents without disrupting the broader infrastructure.

The architecture is built around four primary roles: the **Registry** (a central authority for issuing credentials and managing revocation lists), the **Proxy** (an edge service that enforces trust policies and relays messages), the **Connector** (a local bridge that handles cryptographic operations for the agent), and the **Agent** itself. This separation of concerns allows AI agents to remain framework-agnostic while benefiting from robust security features like proof-of-possession (PoP).

Key technical components include the **Agent Identity Token (AIT)**—a signed JWT that binds an agent's Decentralized Identifier (DID) to its public key—and a bilateral trust establishment process known as a **pairing ceremony**. Communication is conducted over authenticated WebSocket relays, ensuring that backend services remain private and "zero-trust" principles are maintained. The protocol specifically mandates the use of Ed25519 for all signing operations and prohibits weaker algorithms, aiming for a high-security baseline for the future of agentic ecosystems.

---

### Considerations for AI Agent Communication and Networking in Enterprise (draft-han-agent-comm-enterprise)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/4a5a2634-8462-44d8-8a33-22be7b0dd97d
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-han-agent-comm-enterprise/
- Tags: AI Agents, Enterprise Networking, Semantic Routing, Agent Discovery, Multi-Agent Cooperation
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This document investigates key technologies for AI agent communication in enterprise scenarios, including identification, registration, capability discovery, and secure collaboration. It proposes an agent identifier and semantic routing mechanism to facilitate trusted access and efficient cooperation among heterogeneous agents.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Mengyao Han (China Unicom) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/hanmy12@chinaunicom.cn)
- Han Zhengxin (China Unicom) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/hanzx21@chinaunicom.cn)

- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)

- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
