- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Interactive HTML version](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-han-agent-comm-enterprise-00) (Documentation)
- [Plain Text Archive](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-han-agent-comm-enterprise-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-han-agent-comm-enterprise/history/) (Other)
- [BibXML Reference](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/bibxml3/draft-han-agent-comm-enterprise-00.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The version 00 draft introduces the concept of an **Agent Private Network** architecture. Its most significant recent contributions include the proposal for **IPv6-based Agent Identifiers** to replace traditional IP addresses for identity, and **Token-aware Routing**, which classifies and prioritizes specific tokens within an AI data stream to decrease response latency. It also formalizes the requirements for an **Agent Gateway**, positioning it as a four-layer stack consisting of Application Service, Orchestration & Control, Agent Connectivity, and Network Communication layers. Recent updates also link this draft to a broader family of work on "Agent-to-Agent" (A2A) collaboration requirements for campus-level scaling.

**Discussion Summary:**

This Internet-Draft, authored by representatives from China Unicom, provides a comprehensive framework for AI agent communication and networking within enterprise and campus environments. As AI evolves toward autonomous and collaborative agents, the document identifies critical infrastructure gaps, specifically the heterogeneity of agent protocols (e.g., ACP, A2A, ANP) and the limitations of traditional IP addressing for massive-scale (million-level) agent connections. 

The proposed solution centers on an **Agent Gateway** architecture, which serves as a hybrid device combining traditional communication gateway reliability with AI cognitive decision-making. Key technical pillars include the introduction of a globally unique **Agent Identifier (ID)** based on IPv6 and a **semantic/agent-aware routing** mechanism. This allows the network to route traffic based on agent skills, intent, and task objectives rather than just physical locations. Additionally, the draft explores "Token-aware Routing" to prioritize critical tokens in AI interactions, aimed at reducing first-packet latency and ensuring deterministic Quality of Service (QoS) for real-time enterprise applications like robotics and emergency response.

---

### Cross App Access (Identity Assertion Authorization Grant) (XAA)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/80e101b2-e888-4e29-9b52-4aa3b7f7f6ab
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-parecki-oauth-identity-assertion-authz-grant/
- Tags: Agents, Security, OAuth 2.0, Authorization Grant, Token Exchange
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-22

Cross App Access (XAA) lets enterprises enable secure agent-to-app and app-to-app access with centralized IT oversight. Instead of scattered integrations and repeated logins, admins decide what connects, enforce security policies, and see exactly what's being accessed. This makes cross-app and cross-agent integrations seamless and scalable across the enterprise.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Aaron Parecki (Okta) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/aaron@parecki.com)
- Karl McGuinness (Okta) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/public@karlmcguinness.com)
- Brian Campbell (Ping Identity) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/brian.d.campbell@gmail.com)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Mailing list discussion](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/browse/oauth/?q=draft-parecki-oauth-identity-assertion-authz-grant) (Mailing List)
- [OAuth Working Group](https://datatracker.ietf.org/wg/oauth/about/) (Working Group)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-parecki-oauth-identity-assertion-authz-grant/history/) (Other)
- [Plain Text version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-parecki-oauth-identity-assertion-authz-grant-05.txt) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The standard has recently transitioned from an individual submission to an official OAuth Working Group item, now designated as `draft-ietf-oauth-identity-assertion-authz-grant-02`. Recent activity in February and March 2026 includes intensive technical debates on the OAuth mailing list regarding **Delegation Chain Splicing**—a security risk where malicious actors might attempt to combine unrelated tokens in a Token Exchange flow. There is also ongoing feedback concerning the "same IdP prohibition," where developers are seeking clarification on how the grant should behave when the application and the target API are managed by the same identity provider. Version 05 of the draft was published in July 2025, providing updated processing rules for error responses and Identity Assertion JWTs.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Identity Assertion Authorization Grant** (often referred to in discussions as ID-JAG) is an OAuth 2.0 extension designed to facilitate cross-app access in enterprise environments. Specifically, it enables an application that has already authenticated a user via an Identity Provider (IdP) to obtain an access token for a third-party API (Resource Server) without requiring the user to re-authenticate or interact with a browser again. 

The mechanism leverages two existing standards: **Token Exchange ([RFC 8693](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc8693))** and the **JWT Profile for OAuth 2.0 Authorization Grants ([RFC 7523](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7523))**. In this flow, an application takes its initial identity assertion (like an OIDC ID Token) and exchanges it at the IdP for a specialized 'Identity Assertion Authorization Grant' JWT. This new JWT is then presented to the third-party Authorization Server to obtain the final access token.

Key architectural benefits include providing a seamless user experience across different applications and services within a common enterprise umbrella while maintaining strong security boundaries between different trust domains. Current working group discussions are focused on refining security considerations, such as preventing delegation chain splicing and clarifying rules for cases where the client and the API share the same IdP.

---

### Delegated Agent Authorization Protocol (DAAP) (DAAP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/fb46968c-6714-4a82-9a63-757aff85ed58
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-mishra-oauth-agent-grants-00
- Tags: OAuth 2.0, AI Agents, Authorization, Decentralized Identifiers (DID), Delegated Authority, JWT
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A model-neutral, framework-agnostic protocol for verifying that a human authorized a specific AI agent to perform actions on their behalf. It utilizes Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs) for agent identity and provides a tamper-evident audit trail for agent activities.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Sanjeev Kumar (Grantex) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/mishra.sanjeev@gmail.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)
- Emilio A. (Z1) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/emiliosanchez)
- Emilio Jiménez (www.yaco.es) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ejimenez)
- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)
- Paul Selkirk — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pselkirk)
- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)
- Sangho Na — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/microamp)
- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)
- Mark Donnelly — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/meadmaker)
- Valery Smyslov — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/smyslov)
- Jacobo Tarragón — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jminuscula)
- Jim Schaad — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimsch)
- Martin Thomson (mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/martinthomson)
- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Internet-Draft Archive (Text)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-mishra-oauth-agent-grants-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [IETF Datatracker Document Page](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-mishra-oauth-agent-grants/) (Documentation)
- [BibXML Reference](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/bibxml3/draft-mishra-oauth-agent-grants-00.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The protocol has progressed from its initial `-00` draft to a `-01` version (as seen in the Datatracker records). Recent updates to the protocol expand its scope significantly beyond the basic grant flow. New features include **budget controls** for managing spending limits, **real-time event streaming**, and a **credential vault** for secure secret storage. Additionally, the metadata now indicates integration support for external policy backends such as **Open Policy Agent (OPA)** and **Cedar**, moving the protocol toward better enterprise policy alignment. The `-01` draft also highlights the inclusion of a multi-agent delegation model specifically designed with "cascade revocation" to handle complex AI agent swarms.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Delegated Agent Authorization Protocol (DAAP) is an open, model-neutral framework designed to govern the autonomous actions of AI agents. Positioned as a specialized extension to OAuth 2.0, DAAP addresses uniquely agentic requirements such as persistent cryptographic identity, multi-agent delegation, and tamper-evident auditing. At its core, the protocol utilizes Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs) for agent identity (specifically the `did:grantex` method) and issues signed JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) as Grant Tokens. 

The protocol introduces several innovative components for AI governance:
- **Agent Identity**: Agents are identified via DIDs that resolve to identity documents containing developer info and declared scopes.
- **Hierarchical Delegation**: Supports a parent agent spawning sub-agents with reduced permission sets, featuring "cascade revocation" where revoking the primary grant nullifies all delegated tokens.
- **Auditability**: Requires a hash-chained, append-only audit trail to provide a verifiable record of agent actions for regulated environments.
- **Policy Engine**: Allows for automated authorization decisions (auto-approve/deny) to handle high-volume agent deployments while maintaining human principal control.
- **Anomaly Detection**: A runtime monitoring system to detect behavioral deviations from established agent activity baselines.

DAAP aims to provide a standardized way to verify that a specific human (the "Principal") authorized a specific AI agent to perform an action on a third-party service, with the ability to revoke that permission in real-time.

---

### Digital Identity Management for AI Agent Communication Protocols (Agent ID Requirements)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/e4639a0a-472c-41d3-9429-53b098e6ae56
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-yl-agent-id-requirements-00.html
- Tags: AI Agents, Digital Identity, Communication Protocols, Identity Management, Interoperability
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A specification defining the requirements for digital identity management within AI agent communication protocols. It aims to establish a secure, interoperable foundation for connecting agents with users, tools, and other entities in future networks.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Kehan Yao (China Mobile) — Author
- Peng Liu (China Mobile) — Author

**Resources & Links:**

- [Datatracker](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-yl-agent-id-requirements/) (Documentation)
- [XML Source](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-yl-agent-id-requirements-00.xml) (Other)

**What's New:**

The most recent development is the publication of **draft-yl-agent-id-requirements-00** on July 1, 2025. This document marks the formal introduction of requirements for AI agent digital identity within the IETF framework. Specifically, it introduces the concept of "User Binding," ensuring that AI agents can be legally and technically linked to their human owners. It also proposes a three-tier authorization model: **Agent Authorization** (self-representation), **Delegation Authorization** (acting for a user), and **User Authorization** (step-up verification where the agent facilitates direct user consent). These updates aim to address security gaps in autonomous agent-to-agent transactions that current identity protocols do not fully cover.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Digital Identity Management for AI Agent Communication Protocols** is a proposed standard aimed at establishing a universal, secure, and interoperable framework for identifying AI agents. As AI agents transition from software assistants to "embodied AI" (such as humanoid robots and autonomous vehicles), there is a critical need for a standardized way to distinguish these entities in a digital context. The draft defines an AI Agent Digital Identity as a representation containing three primary components: a unique identifier, specific attributes (skills and capabilities), and key credentials for security.

Key functional requirements outlined in the documentation include:
*   **Global Uniqueness:** Agents must possess a globally unique identifier for dynamic location and identification across different network domains.
*   **User Binding:** Because agents often act on behalf of humans, their identity must support links to an associated user to facilitate authorization.
*   **Skill and Capability Mapping:** The standard seeks to define multi-modal capabilities (voice, text, video) and specific "skills" that can be verified and utilized during agent-to-agent or agent-to-human communication.
*   **Security Frameworks:** The standard addresses complex authorization models, including "On-Behalf-Of" (OBO) scenarios where an agent might need to prove delegation from a human user or another agent.
*   **Discovery Mechanisms:** Requirements for registration and synchronization across intra-domain and inter-domain repositories to allow agents to find and interact with one another autonomously.

The initiative bridges existing definitions from the ITU-T and 3GPP, seeking to create a cross-industry foundation for the "new citizens" of future networks. This protocol is intended to replace or augment current GUI-based human-machine interactions with autonomous, intent-based communication.

---

### DNS-Native AI Agent Naming and Resolution (DN-ANR)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/2ea01819-3921-4954-8164-3ad0e5ae6495
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-cui-dns-native-agent-naming-resolution/
- Tags: AI Agents, DNS, Naming Resolution, DNSSEC, FQDN
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

DN-ANR specifies a system for using Fully Qualified Domain Names (FQDNs) as stable identifiers for AI agents, allowing them to resolve to verifiable endpoints via DNSSEC. It provides minimal indexing capabilities to be used by upper-layer discovery systems without carrying heavy semantic metadata in DNS.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Yong Cui (Tsinghua University) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/cuiyong@tsinghua.edu.cn)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)
- Emilio A. (Z1) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/emiliosanchez)
- Emilio Jiménez (www.yaco.es) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ejimenez)
- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)
- Paul Selkirk — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pselkirk)
- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)

- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-cui-dns-native-agent-naming-resolution-01.html) (Primary Spec)
- [Primary Specification (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-cui-dns-native-agent-naming-resolution-01.txt) (Other)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-cui-dns-native-agent-naming-resolution/history/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The latest update (**draft-cui-dns-native-agent-naming-resolution-01**, released March 2, 2026) introduces several significant refinements to the resolution architecture. Most notably, it adds a detailed **HTTPS Fallback Mechanism** utilizing an `agent-dns.json` file and a specific JSON schema to support environments where DNS queries might be restricted or require mirroring. 

The update also expands the **Security section**, providing a more robust specification for **Signature-based Security** and public key requirements. New technical details for **SVCB Private Parameters** and ALPN (Application-Layer Protocol Negotiation) usage have been added to better handle version-specific protocol resolution. Additionally, the draft now includes an **Implementation Checklist** for both Agent Publishers and Client Developers to facilitate easier adoption of the standard.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **DNS-Native AI Agent Naming and Resolution (DN-ANR)** standard establishes a lightweight, DNS-based infrastructure for identifying and connecting to AI agents. The specification aims to provide a stable naming convention using Fully Qualified Domain Names (FQDNs), enabling secure resolution to verifiable endpoints while maintaining strict separation from higher-level discovery or semantic matching systems.

Key technical components include the use of **SVCB (Service Binding)** and **HTTPS Resource Records** for protocol negotiation and version distribution. DN-ANR mandates minimal data in the DNS to avoid "heavy" metadata, focusing instead on cryptographic integrity—ideally through **DNSSEC**. It supports a multi-layered architecture where a discovery layer identifies candidate FQDNs, the resolution layer (defined by this standard) provides connection material, and the connection layer executes session logic (e.g., A2A or MCP protocols).

Current design principles emphasize a "DNS-First" approach, where DNS is the authoritative source and HTTPS serves only as a fallback mirror via a standardized `agent-dns.json` file. The standard also addresses performance through address hints and TTL guidance to ensure deterministic and efficient agent-to-agent communication. Security is a primary focus, featuring optional but recommended signature-based mechanisms and integrity digests to prevent unauthorized redirection or spoofing of agent endpoints.

---

### Enhanced A2A Requirements for Agents Collobration in Enterprise (A2A)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/59e1239d-148f-4adf-94e7-1caf472b0e04
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-zgsgl-dispatch-a2a-requirements-enterprise/
- Tags: Agent-to-Agent, A2A, Enterprise AI, Agent Collaboration, Protocol Requirements
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This document proposes enhanced requirements for the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol specifically tailored for enterprise applications. It focuses on facilitating collaboration between autonomous agents within an enterprise environment.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Li Zhang (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/zhangli344@huawei.com)
- Nan Geng (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/gengnan@huawei.com)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-zgsgl-dispatch-a2a-requirements-enterprise/history/) (Other)
- [Text Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-zgsgl-dispatch-a2a-requirements-enterprise-01.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-zgsgl-dispatch-a2a-requirements-enterprise-01.html) (Documentation)
- [Email Expansions](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-zgsgl-dispatch-a2a-requirements-enterprise/email/) (Mailing List)

**What's New:**

The latest update, version **-01** (released November 28, 2025), focuses on formalized requirements for **centralized directory services** and **temporary collaboration groups**. It specifically addresses the scalability challenges of managing hundreds to thousands of specialized agents within a single enterprise. Recent additions emphasize the need for "intent-based discovery," which allows systems to map complex user prompts to specific combinations of agents. Furthermore, the draft now explicitly calls for **Task Progress Reporting**, enabling remote agents to report execution percentages to maintain visibility over long-running business processes.

**Discussion Summary:**

The "Enhanced A2A Requirements for Agents Collaboration in Enterprise" is an informational Internet-Draft aimed at extending the Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol to meet the specific demands of enterprise environments. While the original A2A protocol (launched by Google Cloud) provides a foundation for AI agents to collaborate, this proposal identifies critical gaps in centralized discovery, complex business process management, and stringent security requirements prevalent in large-scale organizations.

Key technical requirements introduced in the draft include:
* **Enhanced Discovery:** Support for centralized directory services to handle thousands of concurrent agents, reducing search latency and enabling intent-based matching that maps high-level user goals to multi-agent chains.
* **Complex Collaboration:** Mechanisms for creating temporary collaboration groups that automatically disband upon task completion, as well as formal conflict resolution rules for handling data inconsistencies between agents.
* **Enterprise Security:** Fine-grained permission controls mapped to data sensitivity levels and task types, alongside mandatory collaboration behavior audit logs to ensure compliance and traceability.

The standard is currently in the early draft stage within the IETF "dispatch" group, intended to provide a unified standard for how specialized AI agents (e.g., finance, R&D, and HR agents) interact within a secure corporate perimeter.

---

### Entity Attestation Token (EAT) Profile for Autonomous AI Agents (EAT-AI)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/8f21739a-98a6-4808-b8f6-fb25363c5d14
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-messous-eat-ai/
- Tags: AI Agents, Attestation, Security, CBOR, JWT, Model Integrity, Provenance
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A profile for the Entity Attestation Token (EAT) to support remote attestation of autonomous AI agents. It specifies standardized claims for attesting AI model integrity, data provenance, and inference-time access policies using CBOR or JSON Web Tokens.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Ayoub MESSOUS (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/ayoub.messous@outlook.com)
- Lionel Morand (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/lionel.morand@huawei.com)
- Peter Chunchi Liu (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/liuchunchi@huawei.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)

- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)

- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)

- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)

- Martin Thomson (mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/martinthomson)
- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-messous-eat-ai/history/) (Other)
- [Plain Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-messous-eat-ai-01.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-messous-eat-ai-01.html) (Documentation)
- [Email Expansions](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-messous-eat-ai/email/) (Mailing List)

**What's New:**

The latest update, **version -01 (February 2026)**, further refines the claim definitions for AI agents. Key additions include the **`ai-sbom-ref`** claim, which provides a link to Software Bill of Materials specifically for AI runtime dependencies. The update also clarifies the use of **cryptographic digests** for model weights and computational graphs, moving from bare byte strings to a structured format to ensure algorithm agility. Furthermore, it expanded on **multi-component attestation**, providing guidance for complex environments like multi-agent platforms where trust must be layered across various software and hardware components.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Entity Attestation Token (EAT) Profile for Autonomous AI Agents** is an informational IETF Internet-Draft that defines a specialized profile of the Entity Attestation Token (RFC 9711). Its primary objective is to enable remote attestation of autonomous AI agents across different domains, ensuring that verifiers can confirm an agent's technical and operational integrity before granting access to resources or data.

The draft addresses a critical security gap in AI deployment: the lack of standardized claims to verify AI-specific artifacts. By leveraging the IETF Remote ATtestation procedureS (RATS) architecture, this profile allows verifiers to assess an agent based on its model integrity (weights and architecture), training provenance (dataset origin and privacy properties), and runtime authorization (allowed APIs and capabilities). This is particularly relevant for autonomous systems where minimal human oversight increases the risk of model tampering or non-compliant data usage.

Key technical components include:
*   **Core Claims**: Standardized fields for both CBOR Web Tokens (CWT) and JSON Web Tokens (JWT) covering model IDs, cryptographic digests of weights/architecture, and references to AI Software Bill of Materials (AI-SBOM).
*   **Privacy Features**: Support for claims like `dp-epsilon` to verify differential privacy levels used during training.
*   **Interoperability**: Includes optional extensions for 5G/6G network functions to align with ETSI ENI and 3GPP architectures, such as network slice-type authorization.
*   **Layered Trust**: Guidelines for handling multi-agent platforms and multi-model agents through composite attestation.

The profile is designed to complement existing software supply chain standards (like SBOMs) rather than replace them, focusing specifically on the verifiable evidence needed for real-time trust decisions.

---

### Framework for AI Agent Networks (ANP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/15a4bfea-0643-4400-a4ad-aa5572c46420
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-zyyhl-agent-networks-framework/
- Tags: AI Agents, Agent Network Protocol, Networking, Interoperability
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This document defines a framework for AI agent networks based on the Agent Network Protocol (ANP). It specifies the basic functions required to support AI agent communication within a trusted domain.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Zhouye (Aliyun) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/zynetzy1@aliyun.com)
- Kehan Yao (China Mobile) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/yaokehan@chinamobile.com)
- Menghan Yu (China Telecom) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/yumh1@chinatelecom.cn)
- Mengyao Han (China Unicom) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/hanmy12@chinaunicom.cn)
- Cheng Li (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/c.l@huawei.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)

- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-zyyhl-agent-networks-framework-01.html) (Primary Spec)
- [Primary Specification (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-zyyhl-agent-networks-framework-01.txt) (Other)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-zyyhl-agent-networks-framework/history/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The latest update (version 01, October 2025) introduces the **Agent Network Protocol (ANP)** as the foundational protocol for the framework. Key additions include a detailed **Abstract Protocol Flow** outlining the interaction between agents and registration/communication servers. It also formalizes the distinction between **Coordinator Agents** and **Execution Agents** and introduces two specific communication options: mediated through an Agent Communication Server or direct peer-to-peer. New sections on multimodal support and semantic-based route resolution have also been integrated to reflect evolving AI capabilities.

**Discussion Summary:**

The "Framework for AI Agent Networks" is an IETF Internet-Draft that defines a structured approach for communication and collaboration between AI agents. The framework is built upon the **Agent Network Protocol (ANP)** and aims to address the growing need for standardized interoperability in multi-agent systems. It specifically targets agents managed within an operator's network, including terminal-side and network-side agents, while excluding cross-domain communication from its current scope.

The framework identifies three primary roles: **AI Agents**, which are autonomous entities executing tasks; **Agent Registration Servers**, which handle capability registration and discovery; and **Agent Communication Servers**, which manage sessions and routing. Key technical components described include digital identifiers for unique agent addressing, multimodal communication support, and agent description documents in both natural language and structured formats. The protocol workflow supports two primary communication options: mediated communication via a server or direct point-to-point interaction between agents after discovery.

Architecturally, the document covers essential network functions such as task coordination (distinguishing between Coordinator and Execution agents), session management with differentiated Quality of Service (QoS) guarantees, and dual routing mechanisms (ID-based and semantic-based resolution). The framework is intended to enable secure collaboration within a trust domain, providing a foundation for wide-area AI agent interconnection.

---

### Further considerations on AI Agent Authentication and Authorization Based on OAuth Extension

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/2a3af462-941c-4ef9-b6c2-c1480cf2987c
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-yao-agent-auth-considerations/
- Tags: AI Agents, Authentication, Authorization, OAuth Extension, Agent Communication Network
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This document proposes extensions to the OAuth model and new workflows designed for AI agent authentication and authorization to support scalable and trustable Agent Communication Networks (ACN). It addresses the specific requirements for verifying identities and permissions of autonomous AI agents within vertical industries.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Kehan Yao (China Mobile) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/yaokehan@chinamobile.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)

- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)

- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)

- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Document Text (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-yao-agent-auth-considerations-01.txt) (Documentation)
- [Document HTML (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-yao-agent-auth-considerations-01.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-yao-agent-auth-considerations/history/) (Other)
- [Document XML](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-yao-agent-auth-considerations-01.xml) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The latest update (version 01) introduces a more detailed architectural model incorporating **API Proxy Servers** (such as MCP servers) to centralize resource requests. It formally categorizes AI agents into **Physical** and **Virtual** types and defines distinct ownership roles: OBO User, OBO Itself, and OBO Other Agents. New additions also include the technical distinction between **Task-wise Domains** (temporary) and **Application Domains** (permanent) for resource management. The workflow for agents acting on behalf of users has been refined to show how multiple authorization grants are gathered by a proxy and returned to the agent in a single consolidated process.

**Discussion Summary:**

This informational document, "Further considerations on AI Agent Authentication and Authorization Based on OAuth Extension," addresses the evolving security requirements of the Agent Communication Network (ACN). As AI agents—both physical (robots) and virtual (software assistants)—become integral to internet infrastructure, the standard OAuth models require extensions to handle the unique delegated authority of these entities.

The primary focus of the document is extending the OAuth 2.0/2.1 framework to support three distinct operational modes for AI agents:
1. **On-Behalf-Of (OBO) User(s):** Where an agent requires user authorization to access protected resources (e.g., a travel assistant accessing a bank account).
2. **On-Behalf-Of (OBO) Itself:** Where the agent acts as a first-class entity with its own independent identity and identification.
3. **On-Behalf-Of (OBO) Other Agent(s):** Where an agent represents another agent that may lack specific capabilities to perform a task.

A significant architectural addition is the integration of **API Proxy Servers**, specifically referencing the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)**. Instead of agents communicating directly with numerous resource owners—which is deemed inefficient in the AI era—the API Proxy Server acts as a middlebox. It aggregates authorization grants and handles API calls on behalf of the agent, simplifying the workflow when multiple resource owners and different privilege levels are involved.

The document also introduces the **Agent Identifier (AID)** as an independent ID for AI agents and explores concepts like "Task-wise Domains" (temporary domains for specific jobs) versus "Application Domains" (durable domains). Security considerations highlighted include agent impersonation during discovery, excessive attribute disclosure, and the risks of token replay in multi-agent environments.

---

### HTTP Message Signatures for automated traffic Architecture (Web Bot Auth Architecture)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/a54cd698-10cb-4272-ba70-88ae0c3bcd39
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-meunier-web-bot-auth-architecture
- Tags: HTTP, Security, Authentication, Bot Detection, Cryptography, Message Signatures
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An architecture for identifying and authenticating automated HTTP traffic using cryptographic message signatures. It allows automated clients to sign outbound requests so that servers can verify their identity with high confidence.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Thibault Meunier (Cloudflare) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/ot-ietf@thibault.uk)
- Sandor Major (Google) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/ietf@sandormajor.com)
- Thibault — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/thibmeu)
- Loganaden Velvindron (cyberstormdotmu) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/loganaden)
- Ethan Heilman (cloudflare) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/EthanHeilman)
- jhoyla — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jhoyla)
- Mot (dotenvx.com) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/motdotla)
- Miguel Landaeta (AWS) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nomadium)
- Ulas Kirazci — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ulaskira)
- Will Hawkins — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/hawkinsw)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)
- Emilio A. (Z1) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/emiliosanchez)
- Emilio Jiménez (www.yaco.es) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ejimenez)
- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)
- Paul Selkirk — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pselkirk)
- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)

- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)
- Sangho Na — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/microamp)
- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)
- Mark Donnelly — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/meadmaker)
- Valery Smyslov — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/smyslov)
- Jacobo Tarragón — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jminuscula)
- Jim Schaad — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimsch)
- Martin Thomson (mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/martinthomson)
- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Mailing List Subscription](https://www.ietf.org/mailman/listinfo/web-bot-auth/) (Mailing List)
- [Mailing List Archive](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/browse/web-bot-auth/) (Mailing List)
- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/thibmeu/http-message-signatures-directory) (Github)
- [Latest Draft Revision](https://thibmeu.github.io/http-message-signatures-directory/draft-meunier-web-bot-auth-architecture.html) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The standard is currently in an active phase of **Requirements and Impact Analysis**. Within the last month (February 2026), the IETF `web-bot-auth` mailing list has seen a surge in activity focused on two critical areas:

1.  **Use Case Definition:** Participants like David Schinazi and Ted Hardie are refining the scope of which "bots" should be covered, focusing on automated agents that require elevated access or recognition.
2.  **Harm Assessment:** A significant thread led by Mark Nottingham is investigating the "Harms" of the proposal, specifically looking at how standardized bot signatures could potentially be used for tracking, fingerprinting, or exclusionary practices by servers.

Technically, the **latest draft revision** (published late 2025) has stabilized the integration with the base HTTP Message Signatures spec, shifting the focus from "how to sign" to the "architecture of trust" between bot owners and web hosts.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **HTTP Message Signatures for Automated Traffic Architecture** is a proposed standard aimed at identifying and authenticating automated web traffic (bots). Developed by Thibault Meunier and Sandor Major, the architecture leverages the existing **HTTP Message Signatures** framework to allow automated clients to cryptographically sign their outbound requests. This mechanism provides HTTP servers with a reliable way to verify the identity of a bot, distinguishing legitimate automated agents (like search engine crawlers or monitoring tools) from malicious or anonymous traffic.

The initiative addresses the long-standing challenge of "Web Bot Authentication," where servers often rely on brittle methods like IP whitelisting or easily spoofed User-Agent strings. By moving to a signature-based model, the standard aims to increase confidence in bot identity while reducing the operational overhead for both bot operators and site administrators. Recent discussions within the IETF `web-bot-auth` mailing list highlight a focus on defining specific use cases, such as search indexers and AI training crawlers, and carefully evaluating the potential "harms" or privacy implications of widely deploying a standardized bot identification framework. Currently, the project is hosted in a dedicated GitHub repository and is undergoing active technical refinement through the IETF community.

---

### OAuth 2.0 Extension: On-Behalf-Of User Authorization for AI Agents (OAuth 2.0 AI Agent Extension)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/fd971cf8-8dca-432b-b351-1d00dd41909b
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-oauth-ai-agents-on-behalf-of-user/
- Tags: OAuth 2.0, AI Agents, Authorization, Delegation, Identity
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This specification extends the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework to enable AI agents to securely obtain access tokens for acting on behalf of users. It introduces parameters to identify the specific agent requiring delegation and ensures secure delegation with explicit user consent and enhanced auditability.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Thilina (Independent) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/thilinasenarath97@gmail.com)
- Ayesha Dissanayaka (Independent) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/ayshsandu@gmail.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)

- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Document Status Page](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-oauth-ai-agents-on-behalf-of-user/) (Documentation)
- [Plain Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-oauth-ai-agents-on-behalf-of-user-02.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-oauth-ai-agents-on-behalf-of-user-02.html) (Documentation)
- [XML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-oauth-ai-agents-on-behalf-of-user-02.xml) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-oauth-ai-agents-on-behalf-of-user/history/) (Other)
- [Email Expansions](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-oauth-ai-agents-on-behalf-of-user/email/) (Mailing List)

**What's New:**

The latest update (**version -02**, released August 26, 2025) refines the protocol flow for AI agent delegation. Key changes include clearer definitions for the **`requested_actor`** and **`actor_token`** parameters. The draft now provides a more detailed sequence for the **Resource Server Challenge**, explaining how a resource server can signal a need for user-delegated authorization via the `WWW-Authenticate` header. Additionally, the update improves the description of the access token structure to ensure it properly documents the delegation chain from the user to the agent via a client application, specifically targeting use cases like **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** servers.

**Discussion Summary:**

This specification extends the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework ([RFC6749](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6749)) to address the specific needs of AI agents acting on behalf of users. While standard OAuth flows and Token Exchange ([RFC8693](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8693)) exist, they often lack mechanisms for explicit user consent via the front channel or fail to distinguish the agent as a unique identity during token exchange.

The extension modifies the **Authorization Code Grant** flow by introducing two primary parameters:
1.  **`requested_actor`**: Used at the authorization endpoint to identify the specific AI agent requiring delegation. This allows the Authorization Server to present a consent screen that explicitly names the agent to the user.
2.  **`actor_token`**: Used at the token endpoint to authenticate the agent when the client exchanges the authorization code for an access token.

The resulting access token is a JSON Web Token (JWT) that captures the full delegation chain, including the user (subject), the client application, and the AI agent (actor). This structure enhances auditability and transparency for resource servers, such as Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, which can then validate the entire delegation path before granting access to protected resources. The flow is designed to be triggered dynamically by a Resource Server challenge (e.g., a 401 Unauthorized response), ensuring consent is obtained at the moment access is required.

---

### OAuth 2.0 Scope Aggregation for Multi-Step AI Agent Workflows

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/ddb6b423-2983-424b-abd1-36d2ce35d6d0
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-jia-oauth-scope-aggregation
- Tags: OAuth 2.0, AI Agents, Authorization, Scope Aggregation, Workflows
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An OAuth 2.0 authorization pattern that allows AI agents to aggregate multiple required scopes into a single authorization procedure. This approach reduces repeated user consent prompts and improves efficiency in multi-step AI agent workflows across different resources.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Yukuan Jia (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/jiayukuan@huawei.com)
- Shuping Peng (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/pengshuping@huawei.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)

- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)

- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)

- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)

- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)

- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)

- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-jia-oauth-scope-aggregation-00) (Primary Spec)
- [Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-jia-oauth-scope-aggregation-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [XML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-jia-oauth-scope-aggregation-00.xml) (Documentation)
- [Web Authorization Protocol (oauth) Working Group](https://datatracker.ietf.org/wg/oauth/about/) (Working Group)

**What's New:**

The specification is in its earliest stage (version 00). The most significant recent development is the formal definition of the `security` member in JSON-based resource metadata. This member includes `type` (e.g., "oauth2"), `scopes`, and `as_metadata` (AS discovery URL). The draft specifically highlights its role in filling gaps in existing protocols like the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)**, which currently lacks a standardized way to advertise per-tool scope requirements, often resulting in reactive, manual authorization triggers.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **OAuth 2.0 Scope Aggregation for Multi-Step AI Agent Workflows** is emerging as a standards-track proposal within the IETF Web Authorization Protocol (OAuth) working group. It addresses a specific efficiency gap in AI agent operations: when an agent executes a plan requiring access to multiple protected resources, traditional OAuth flows often trigger individual authorization challenges for each resource. This leads to "user fatigue" through repeated consent prompts and increased network latency.

The specification introduces a proactive authorization pattern. By aggregating all required scopes across an entire workflow into a single authorization request, the AI agent can obtain one comprehensive access token (or a set of tokens) up-front. A key technical contribution of this draft is the extension of **Resource Metadata** with a new `security` member. This allows resource servers to advertise their required OAuth scopes and authorization server (AS) metadata. With this information, an AI agent can perform "Scope Aggregation" by calculating the minimal set of permissions needed across its planned steps before execution begins.

Key architectural concepts include **Authorization Domains**, which are logical groupings of scopes authorizable by a single trust anchor. The draft also emphasizes backward compatibility, allowing agents to fall back to reactive "step-up" authorization if the proactive aggregation metadata is unavailable. While improving efficiency, the standard notes security risks such as "Residual Privilege" (excessive permissions if a workflow is partially executed) and "User Blind Signing" (users consenting to long lists of scopes they may not fully understand).

---

### OAuth Entity Profiles for Model-Oriented Resource Access (MORA) (MORA)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/11a2e753-9a82-477e-83c7-60f9c06fd3cf
- Organization: Anthropic
- Specification: https://github.com/Sreyanth/draft-mora-oauth-entity-profiles
- Tags: OAuth, AI Identity, Model Access Control, MORA, Authentication
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

OAuth Entity Profiles for Model-Oriented Resource Access (MORA) establishes a framework for identifying and authenticating different entities (like AI models and developers) within an OAuth flow. It specifically addresses how AI models can be uniquely identified and authorized to access resources.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Sreyanth Reddy Mamilla (Anthropic) — Author
- Sreyanth (Microsoft) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Sreyanth)

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/Sreyanth/draft-mora-oauth-entity-profiles) (Github)

**What's New:**

Recent activity in the MORA standard development focuses on refining the **Entity Profiles** to better distinguish between the "Primary Client" (the application the user interacts with) and the "AI Agent" (the model performing the processing). Recent updates to the GitHub repository (as of late 2024) include expanded sections on **Proof of Possession (PoP)** tokens to ensure that AI agents cannot reuse intercepted tokens. There is also increased focus on defining "Inference Scopes," which limit the AI's ability to only perform specific actions, such as "summarization only" or "read-only analysis," preventing the model from taking unauthorized destructive actions.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **OAuth Entity Profiles for Model-Oriented Resource Access (MORA)** is an emerging security standard designed to address the unique authorization challenges posed by Large Language Models (LLMs) and AI agents. As AI agents increasingly act on behalf of users to access web resources, traditional OAuth flows struggle with the "delegation's delegation" problem. MORA introduces specific entity profiles (Client, Resource Server, and Authorization Server) to standardize how AI models request, receive, and prove authorization for resource access.

The core objective is to provide a structured framework where AI models can be identified and constrained through fine-grained access tokens. This prevents "confused deputy" attacks and ensures that when an AI agent accesses a user's data (e.g., email or calendar), it does so within a verifiable and limited scope. The standard is currently in the early draft stages, with active development focused on defining the interactions between the AI "Consumer" and the data "Provider."

Key technical components include:
- **Entity Identification:** Distinct ways to identify the AI model vs. the application using it.
- **Model-Specific Scopes:** Tailoring OAuth scopes to be interpretable by or restrictive to automated agents.
- **Token Bindings:** Ensuring access tokens are cryptographically bound to the specific AI entity performing the task.

---

### OAuth Identity and Authorization Chaining Across Domains (OAuth Identity Chaining)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/d9156e41-2b8a-496f-9577-2766de5c6b41
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining-05
- Tags: OAuth 2.0, Identity, Authorization, Security, Trust Domains, Identity Chaining
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This specification defines a mechanism to preserve identity and authorization information across trust domains that use the OAuth 2.0 Framework. It enables secure propagation of user context and permissions through multiple interconnected domains.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Arndt Schwenkschuster (SPIRL) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/arndts.ietf@gmail.com)
- Pieter Kasselman (SPIRL) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/prkasselman@gmail.com)
- Kelley Burgin (MITRE) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/kburgin@mitre.org)
- Michael J. Jenkins (NSA) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/mjjenki@cyber.nsa.gov)
- Brian Campbell (Ping Identity) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/brian.d.campbell@gmail.com)
- PieterKas (SPIRL) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PieterKas)
- Aaron Parecki (okta) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aaronpk)
- Dean H. Saxe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/deansaxe)

- Kamron Batman — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kamronbatman)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)
- Emilio A. (Z1) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/emiliosanchez)

- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)
- Paul Selkirk — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pselkirk)
- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)
- Sangho Na — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/microamp)
- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)
- Mark Donnelly — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/meadmaker)
- Valery Smyslov — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/smyslov)
- Jacobo Tarragón — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jminuscula)
- Jim Schaad — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimsch)
- Martin Thomson (mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/martinthomson)
- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository and Issue Tracker](https://github.com/oauth-wg/oauth-identity-chaining) (Github)
- [Web Authorization Protocol Working Group mailing list archive](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/browse/oauth/) (Mailing List)
- [OAuth (oauth) Working Group Page](https://datatracker.ietf.org/wg/oauth/about/) (Working Group)
- [Plain Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining-05.txt) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The latest update, **version 05**, was released in July 2025. This iteration refines the processing rules for token exchanges and provides clearer examples for the **JWT Authorization Grant** flow. Significant focus has been placed on **Claims Transcription**, ensuring that when an identity moves from "Domain A" to "Domain B," the semantics of the authorization remain intact. Recent discussions in the OAuth Working Group (as of March 2026) show a broader push to finalize Best Current Practices (BCPs) for JWTs (rfc8725bis), which directly impacts how identity chaining is implemented securely. The draft has also matured its metadata section, defining how Authorization Servers should advertise their ability to participate in an identity chain.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **OAuth Identity and Authorization Chaining Across Domains** standard is an IETF Internet-Draft (draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining) developed by the Web Authorization Protocol (OAuth) Working Group. Its primary objective is to define a robust mechanism for preserving identity and authorization information across different trust domains that utilize the OAuth 2.0 Framework. This is particularly relevant in complex environments where a service in one domain needs to access resources in another domain while maintaining the original context of the user or client identity.

The specification leverages existing OAuth mechanisms, specifically **Token Exchange (RFC 8693)** and **JWT Profile for OAuth 2.0 Client Authentication and Authorization Grants (RFC 7523)**, to bridge these domains. Key technical components include:
*   **Authorization Server Discovery:** Guidance on how clients find the appropriate authorization server in a foreign domain.
*   **Token Exchange Protocols:** Detailed request and response structures for exchanging tokens between domains.
*   **JWT Authorization Grants:** Using JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) as authorization grants to carry identity information across boundaries.
*   **Claims Transcription:** Defining how claims from an initial token are mapped or transcribed into a new token suitable for the target domain.
*   **Metadata:** New Authorization Server metadata parameters to indicate support for identity chaining.

The draft is currently in the 'Informational' track, meaning it aims to provide best practices and standardized patterns rather than defining a mandatory core protocol change. It is designed to solve the "broken chain" problem in microservices or federated architectures where identity context often gets lost when crossing organizational or security boundaries.

---

### OAuth2.0 Extension for Multi-AI Agent Collaboration (OAuth2.0-AI-Agent)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/59ead549-5128-48cb-9de8-dcd84c088a0b
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-song-oauth-ai-agent-collaborate-authz
- Tags: OAuth 2.0, AI Agents, Multi-Agent Collaboration, Authorization, Agentic AI
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An extension to the OAuth 2.0 protocol designed for task-oriented multi-AI agent collaboration. It enables a lead agent to coordinate sub-agents as a task group by adding specific fields in token and message flows, streamlining the authorization process.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Yurong Song (Huawei) — Author
- LUN LI (Huawei) — Author
- Yuning Jiang (Huawei) — Author
- Faye Liu (Huawei) — Author
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)
- Emilio A. (Z1) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/emiliosanchez)
- Emilio Jiménez (www.yaco.es) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ejimenez)
- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)
- Paul Selkirk — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pselkirk)
- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
