**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-song-oauth-ai-agent-collaborate-authz-01) (Primary Spec)
- [Primary Specification (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-song-oauth-ai-agent-collaborate-authz-01.txt) (Other)
- [Web Authorization Protocol (OAuth) Working Group](https://datatracker.ietf.org/wg/oauth/about/) (Working Group)
- [Document History/Feed](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-song-oauth-ai-agent-collaborate-authz/history/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The latest update, **version 01 (released February 2026)**, transitions the draft into an "Experimental" status. Key updates include a clearer distinction between Static and Dynamic task group authorization procedures. The revision refines the roles of the 'Applier' (leading agent) and 'Clients' (sub-agents) and clarifies the extension of the Client Registration process. Specifically, it introduces the requirement for a "capability" parameter in the leading agent's profile (e.g., "resolve intent and distribute tasks") to validate authorization requests. It also provides detailed examples of the access token structure, featuring the `app` claim and multiple `sbj`-`aud`-`scope` pairs to support task groups.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **OAuth 2.0 Extension for Multi-AI Agent Collaboration** is an Internet-Draft proposing a specialized authorization framework for scenarios where multiple AI agents collaborate on complex tasks. In these environments, a "leading agent" coordinates several "sub-agents." To avoid the inefficiency of each sub-agent independently requesting tokens from an Authorization Server (AS), this extension allows a leading agent to act as an "Applier," requesting access tokens on behalf of the entire task group.

The specification identifies two primary collaboration policies:
1.  **Static Policy:** All tasks and sub-agents are determined upfront. The leading agent requests a consolidated access token for the group before execution.
2.  **Dynamic Policy:** Tasks and sub-agents are identified incrementally. The leading agent applies for tokens as needed and issues bound task credentials to sub-agents.

Key technical additions include new fields in the token and message flows, such as `applier_id` (used during the request) and `app` (a claim within the access token identifying the applier). It also introduces a new error code, `unauthorized_applier`, for cases where an agent lacks the registered capability to distribute tasks. The framework is designed to be fully backward-compatible with existing OAuth 2.0 workflows while supporting privacy mechanisms like selective disclosure for token transmission.

---

### OAuth2.0 Extention for AI Agent: Authorization on Target (OAuth2-AI-Agent)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/2204a9a1-dde9-4330-ab82-64035af40443
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-song-oauth-ai-agent-authorization/
- Tags: OAuth 2.0, AI Agent, Authorization, Security, Identity
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An extension to the OAuth 2.0 protocol that introduces a target_id field for granular authorization of AI agents. It aims to support precise permission management and enhance traceability for virtual or physical AI components in a network.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Yurong Song (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/songyurong1@huawei.com)
- LUN LI (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/lilun20@huawei.com)
- Faye Liu (Individual) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/feyerliu@gmail.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)

- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Current Draft (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-song-oauth-ai-agent-authorization-00.html) (Documentation)
- [Current Draft (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-song-oauth-ai-agent-authorization-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-song-oauth-ai-agent-authorization/history/) (Other)
- [Email Expansions](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-song-oauth-ai-agent-authorization/email/) (Mailing List)

**What's New:**

The standard is currently in its first iteration (**version -00**), published on **July 3, 2025**. This release introduces the foundational concepts of a "Target" (AI agent/model) and the the `target_id` parameter. Key recent updates in this draft include the definition of two specific use cases: virtual AI agents (models as software modules) and physical AI agents (models hosted on agent hardware). It also introduces the `tar` claim for access tokens, allowing Authorization Servers to specify the authorized target in the final credential.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **OAuth 2.0 Extension for AI Agent: Authorization on Target** is an IETF Internet-Draft that proposes a granular authorization mechanism specifically for AI agents and AI models. It addresses the unique characteristics of AI agents, such as autonomy and long-lived identities, which are not fully covered by standard OAuth 2.0 workflows. The core of the proposal is the introduction of a **Target**—a specific module, virtual agent, or physical model within a client that requires authorization.

The extension introduces an optional field, `target_id`, into the OAuth 2.0 protocol flow. This allows a client to explicitly identify which specific AI component is requesting access to a resource. This granularity is intended to enhance security by preventing unauthorized or malicious behavior from specific AI components without disrupting the entire client's operation. It also supports regulatory compliance (like GDPR) by ensuring resource owners know exactly which AI entity is utilizing their data. 

The draft specifies extensions for:
* **Client Registration:** Including AI agent/model identities and capabilities in the client profile.
* **Authorization Requests:** Enabling the client to select the correct "target" based on task requirements or resource availability.
* **Access Token Requests:** Including the `target_id` in the token request and a corresponding `tar` claim in the issued access token to finalize the granular permission.

---

### Open Agent Identity Specification (OAI-1)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/b4f58ed2-3635-40b9-9e0f-4ed687e873d7
- Organization: Open Agent Identity Foundation
- Specification: https://openagentidentity.org
- Tags: identity, discovery, AI agents, decentralized identity constellations, interoperability, DNS, MCP
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A decentralized standard for discovering, verifying, and interacting with AI agents using DNS-rooted identity. It provides a universal identity foundation that enables interoperability between different agent protocols like MCP, A2A, and UCP.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Sierra (Sierra) — Contributor
- Decagon (Decagon) — Contributor
- Open Agent Identity Working Group (Open Agent Identity Foundation) — Author

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification](https://openagentidentity.org) (Primary Spec)
- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/openagentidentity/spec) (Github)
- [Reference Manifest](https://acme-agent.com/.well-known/agent-identity.json) (Documentation)
- [Reference Implementation](https://acme-agent.com) (Reference Impl)

**What's New:**

### Recent Updates (February 2026)
The specification has recently advanced to **Version 1.0.5 (Draft)**. Key recent activities include:
*   **Improved Reference Implementation:** The release of the "Acme" interactive tutorial and reference agent (v1.0.3) providing a "learn-by-doing" environment for developers.
*   **Enhanced Manifest Features:** The `agent-identity.json` now includes detailed `capabilities` and `pricing` fields, allowing agents to advertise specific tools (e.g., `validate_manifest`, `check_dns`) and their cost structure (currently defaulting to "free" in reference models).
*   **MCP Integration:** Formalized documentation on integrating OAI with the Model Context Protocol to standardize how identified agents expose their functional tools to clients.
*   **Public Key Infrastructure:** Standardized usage of **Ed25519** public keys within the manifest for secure identity anchoring.

**Discussion Summary:**

### Overview
The **Open Agent Identity (OAI)** specification is a decentralized protocol designed for the discovery, verification, and interaction of AI Agents. It addresses the need for a standardized identity layer in the burgeoning AI agent ecosystem, enabling agents to represent themselves securely and allowing clients to verify agent provenance before engaging in transactions or data exchange.

### Key Components and Decisions
*   **Identity & Discovery:** Agents are identified via a decentralized standard. Discovery relies on an `agent-identity.json` manifest file hosted at a `/.well-known/` path on the agent's domain.
*   **Agent Manifest:** A structured JSON object that defines the agent's version, identity (name, handle, public key), operator details (contact, privacy policy), and capabilities (tools, permissions).
*   **Protocol Compatibility:** The standard explicitly supports integration with the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)**, facilitating a bridge between identity and standardized execution of agentic tools.
*   **Security & Verification:** Identity is anchored by DNS verification and Ed25519 public keys. The protocol includes mechanisms for client authentication and authorization policies (e.g., "open" vs. "restricted").
*   **Commerce & Tools:** The specification includes a `pricing` field in the manifest to support zero-touch payments and detailed `capabilities` definitions for tool-calling.

### Open Questions and Deadlines
The current version (1.0.5) is marked as a **Draft**. While the manifest structure is maturing, details regarding the full extent of "zero-touch payments" and specific cross-chain or cross-platform payment implementations remain areas for further refinement. The specification is actively seeking feedback via its GitHub repository.

---

### Policy and Lifecycle Extensions for OAuth Rich Authorization Requests (RAR-AGENT-EXTENSIONS)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/47900c17-640d-4755-86a8-efde0c5aa6e3
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-chen-oauth-rar-agent-extensions-00
- Tags: OAuth 2.0, RAR, AI Agents, Authorization, Security, Lifecycle Management
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Extends OAuth 2.0 Rich Authorization Requests (RAR) with policy context and lifecycle binding to support AI agent ecosystems. It introduces mechanisms to request specific security assurance levels and to bind authorization validity to the lifecycle of automated tasks.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Meiling Chen (China Mobile) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/chenmeiling90@hotmail.com)
- Li Su (China Mobile) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/suli@chinamobile.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)

- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)

- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)
- Sangho Na — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/microamp)
- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)
- Mark Donnelly — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/meadmaker)

- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-chen-oauth-rar-agent-extensions-00) (Primary Spec)
- [Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-chen-oauth-rar-agent-extensions-00.txt) (Other)
- [XML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-chen-oauth-rar-agent-extensions-00.xml) (Other)
- [OAuth Working Group](https://datatracker.ietf.org/wg/oauth/about/) (Working Group)

**What's New:**

The standard is currently in its very first iteration as an **Individual Internet-Draft** (version 00), published in February 2026. The most recent updates include the formal definitions for the `policy_context` and `lifecycle_binding` JSON objects. Recent work focuses on specifying how Authorization Servers should process these new fields—including cross-validation of policy levels against requested actions—and how Resource Servers should handle real-time revocation when tokens are bound to external lifecycles. The draft also introduced a new IANA registry for "OAuth Policy Compliance Frameworks."

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Policy and Lifecycle Extensions for OAuth Rich Authorization Requests** is a proposed extension to the Rich Authorization Requests (RAR) framework defined in RFC 9396. The standard addresses emerging security and operational challenges found in complex AI agent ecosystems and automated distributed systems. Specifically, it introduces two new optional members to the `authorization_details` object: `policy_context` and `lifecycle_binding`.

The `policy_context` member allows clients to explicitly request a specific security assurance level (e.g., financial-grade or HIPAA compliance) for an authorization decision. This is designed to prevent "policy downgrade attacks," where a high-privilege action might otherwise be granted under a low-assurance default policy. It enables Authorization Servers (AS) to execute specific evaluation rules, such as mandatory multi-factor authentication (MFA) or specialized risk analysis, based on the requested context.

The `lifecycle_binding` member addresses the risk of excessive permission lifetimes. In traditional OAuth, access tokens rely on a fixed expiration time (`exp`). This extension allows an authorization grant's validity to be tied to the status of an external entity or task (e.g., a specific data processing job). By using mechanisms like webhooks, the AS can immediately revoke a token as soon as the associated task completes, fails, or is cancelled, adhering to the principle of least privilege.

The standard also defines new Authorization Server metadata parameters (`policy_assurance_levels_supported` and `policy_compliance_frameworks_supported`) to allow clients to discover supported security levels, and introduces a new error code, `policy_requirement_not_met`, for cases where the requested context cannot be satisfied.

---

### Problem Statement: Network-Layer Infrastructure for Autonomous Agent Communication (PILOT)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/cb535730-eb66-43d1-8ce8-b0d8a6079d31
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-teodor-pilot-problem-statement/
- Tags: Autonomous Agents, Network Infrastructure, Agent Communication, AI Identity, Internet-Draft
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This document describes the requirement for a network-layer infrastructure that provides autonomous agents with first-class network citizenship. It addresses the limitations of current agent communication protocols that rely on centralized application-layer infrastructure and lack native network-layer identity, addressing, and transport.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Calin Teodor (Vulture Labs) — Author [profile](/person/calin@vulturelabs.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)

- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Text version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-teodor-pilot-problem-statement-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-teodor-pilot-problem-statement-00.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-teodor-pilot-problem-statement/history/) (Other)
- [IETF Tracker Issue/Bug Report](https://github.com/ietf-tools/datatracker/issues/new/choose) (Github)

**What's New:**

The most recent development is the release of **draft-teodor-pilot-problem-statement-00** on March 14, 2026. This draft represents a shift in IETF focus from application-layer agent protocols (like MCP/A2A) toward the **network and transport layers**. It introduces the concept of a "PILOT" infrastructure aimed at providing virtual addressing and NAT traversal specifically for AI agents. Notably, the draft claims that approximately 88% of real-world environments involve NAT, making existing HTTP-centric agent communication impractical for edge-based or mobile agents. It also proposes a **privacy-by-default** discovery model to prevent mass enumeration of agent endpoints.

**Discussion Summary:**

This standard document, "Problem Statement: Network-Layer Infrastructure for Autonomous Agent Communication," identifies a critical gap in the current Internet protocol stack regarding AI agents. While application-layer protocols like the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol define how agents communicate, the document argues that these assume the presence of stable, human-centric web infrastructure (DNS, stable IP addresses, and HTTP endpoints). The core problem is that AI agents are often transient, run behind NAT, migrate between hosts, and lack persistent network-layer identities.

The proposed standard seeks to define requirements for a "PILOT" network-layer infrastructure that grants agents "first-class network citizenship." Key issues identified include the coupling of agent identity to infrastructure, the lack of peer-to-peer communication without complex web intermediaries, and the absence of an agent-native trust model (beyond human-centric models like TOFU or expensive CA-based TLS). The document proposes an overlay network approach using virtual addressing to decouple identity from location, similar to the logic of LISP (RFC 9300).

The draft outlines several technical requirements:
* **Virtual Addressing:** Stable identifiers independent of the physical network attachment.
* **NAT Traversal:** Automatic and transparent P2P communication for agents on edge devices.
* **Bilateral Trust:** A model where mutual consent is required before any communication can occur.
* **Privacy-by-Default:** Agents remain invisible until they explicitly authorize a relationship.

---

### Registry and Signature Agent card for Web bot auth (WebBotAuth Registry)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/09cef729-a711-4460-a8c3-b65f64d0567b
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-meunier-webbotauth-registry/
- Tags: Web Bot, Authentication, JSON, IANA Registry, Signature Agent, Metadata
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This document defines a JSON-based 'Signature Agent Card' format for web agents to advertise metadata including identity, purpose, and cryptographic keys. It also establishes an IANA registry for these parameters to enable extensible and interoperable discovery of agent information.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Maxime Guerreiro (Cloudflare) — Author
- Ulas Kirazci (Amazon) — Author
- Thibault Meunier (Cloudflare) — Author

**Resources & Links:**

- [Internet-Draft (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-meunier-webbotauth-registry-01.txt) (Documentation)
- [Internet-Draft (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-meunier-webbotauth-registry-01.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-meunier-webbotauth-registry/history/) (Other)
- [Email Expansions](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-meunier-webbotauth-registry/email/) (Mailing List)

**What's New:**

The latest revision (**01**) of the draft, published in October 2025, refines the **Signature Agent Card** JSON structure and clarifies the discovery process. Notable updates include the formalization of parameters for **robots.txt compliance** (`rfc9309-compliance`) and **rate-expectation** (e.g., `avg=10rps;max=100rps`). The draft also introduces a more detailed **Discovery** section using ABNF syntax to define registry formats and supports the use of `https`, `http`, and `data` URI schemes for accessing agent cards. Discussion has been revitalized within the **Web Bot Auth Working Group** to address outstanding "TODO" items regarding signature formats and rate control specifications.

**Discussion Summary:**

The "Registry and Signature Agent card for Web bot auth" is a proposed informational standard designed to formalize how automated web clients (bots) identify themselves to origin servers. Currently, bot identification is inconsistent, relying on a mix of User-Agent strings, static IP lists, or external documentation. This draft introduces the **Signature Agent Card**, a JSON-based format that allows agents to advertise metadata including identity, purpose, contact information, rate expectations, and cryptographic keys.

Key components of the standard include:
*   **Signature Agent Card Parameters**: A set of JSON fields such as `client_name`, `purpose` (e.g., search, TDM), `expected-user-agent`, and `rate-expectation`.
*   **Cryptographic Integration**: The card includes a `keys` parameter containing a JSON Web Key Set (JWKS), enabling servers to verify signed HTTP requests from the bot.
*   **Discovery Mechanisms**: The document defines how registries (lists of URLs pointing to agent cards) can be published and accessed using HTTPS or data URIs.
*   **IANA Registry**: The draft proposes the creation of an IANA registry for "Signature Agent Card Parameters" to ensure the format remains extensible and interoperable.

By providing a structured way for bots to declare their intent and operational constraints (like `robots.txt` compliance and rate control), the standard aims to improve trust and reduce the friction between bot operators and website administrators.

---

### SCIM Agents and Agentic Applications Extension (SCIM Agent Extension)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/4d0fa0cd-4b02-42d6-8fc8-130b2348391d
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-abbey-scim-agent-extension-00.html
- Tags: SCIM, Identity Management, AI Agents, Agentic Applications, Workloads, Interoperability
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This document describes a SCIM 2.0 extension for agents and agentic applications, including extensions to core User and Group objects and new resource types. It aims to provide interoperability between identity providers and agentic constructs while reducing the need for new specialized protocols.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Macy Abbey (Okta) — Author
- Rafael S. Cohen (Okta) — Author

**Resources & Links:**

- [Internet-Draft HTML](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-abbey-scim-agent-extension-00.html) (Primary Spec)
- [RFC XML Source](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-abbey-scim-agent-extension-00.xml) (Other)
- [SCIM Working Group](https://datatracker.ietf.org/wg/scim/about/) (Working Group)

**What's New:**

The extension is currently in its first iteration (**draft-00**). The most recent updates focus on defining the core `Agent` resource type and the mechanisms for SCIM Service Providers to advertise support via `ServiceProviderConfig`. 

Key recent additions include:
*   **Agent Filtering**: Guidance for service providers to implement filtering on `name` and `externalId`.
*   **Protocol Support**: Inclusion of a `protocols` multi-valued attribute to describe how an agent can be reached (supporting agent-to-agent or human-to-agent communication).
*   **Relationship Mapping**: Explicitly linking agents to "Agentic Applications" to define trust boundaries.
*   **Reference to PAM**: Integration with `draft-grizzle-scim-pam-ext` for backward compatibility when dedicated agent resources aren't available.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **SCIM Agents and Agentic Applications Extension** is a proposed extension to the System for Cross-domain Identity Management (SCIM) 2.0 protocol. Its primary objective is to enable the management of AI agents and agentic applications using existing SCIM infrastructure, thereby reducing the need for new, complex identity protocols in the AI space.

The extension introduces several key components:
*   **New Resource Types**: It defines a dedicated `Agent` resource type, distinct from traditional users or software workloads. This reflects the unique nature of agents which may exhibit unpredictable behavior due to their reliance on AI models.
*   **Schema Extensions**: It provides extensions to the `ServiceProviderConfig` to allow SCIM servers to advertise support for agents and agentic applications.
*   **Agent Attributes**: The proposed `Agent` schema includes standard identity attributes (id, name, externalId) alongside agent-specific fields such as `agentType`, `protocols` (for communication capabilities), and `applications` (to define trust boundaries).
*   **Interoperability**: The draft suggests that if a server does not yet support the dedicated agent resource, clients should link agent-related User objects using the `LinkedObject` pattern from the PAM extension (`draft-grizzle-scim-pam-ext-01`).

By leveraging SCIM, the standard aims to provide a stable path for cross-domain agent management and discovery, allowing emerging AI standards to focus on novel functionalities rather than identity plumbing.

---

### SD Agent: Selective Disclosure for Agent Discovery and Identity Management (Agent-SD-JWT)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/df514eab-bb74-48c0-9ed6-a87604b927d0
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-nandakumar-agent-sd-jwt-02
- Tags: SD-JWT, A2A, Agent Discovery, Identity Management, Privacy, Model Context Protocol
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Integrates Selective Disclosure for JWTs (SD-JWT) with Agent-to-Agent (A2A) systems to provide privacy-preserving agent discovery and identity management. It specifies the SD-Card format to enable selective disclosure of agent capabilities, contact information, and metadata.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Suhas Nandakumar (Cisco) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/snandaku@cisco.com)
- Cullen Fluffy Jennings (Cisco) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/fluffy@iii.ca)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)
- Emilio A. (Z1) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/emiliosanchez)
- Emilio Jiménez (www.yaco.es) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ejimenez)
- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)
- Paul Selkirk — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pselkirk)
- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)
- Sangho Na — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/microamp)
- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)
- Mark Donnelly — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/meadmaker)
- Valery Smyslov — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/smyslov)
- Jacobo Tarragón — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jminuscula)
- Jim Schaad — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimsch)
- Martin Thomson (mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/martinthomson)
- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Datatracker Page](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-nandakumar-agent-sd-jwt/02/) (Documentation)
- [Plain Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-nandakumar-agent-sd-jwt-02.txt) (Documentation)
- [XML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-nandakumar-agent-sd-jwt-02.xml) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The latest update (version 02) introduces the **SD-Card** format, specifically tailored for the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) ecosystem. Key additions include:
*   **Selective Disclosure Claims:** Specific definitions for hiding/revealing agent skills, capabilities, security schemes, and provider information.
*   **Key Binding Support:** Integration of the `cnf` (confirmation) claim to cryptographically bind an agent's identity to a public/private key pair.
*   **Discovery Protocols:** New guidance on privacy-protected discovery via Well-Known URIs and Registry-based lookups.
*   **System Architecture:** A formalized four-party model (Registry, Agent, Service, Client) for managing disclosable credentials.
*   **IANA Registrations:** Initial requests for media types (e.g., `application/vnd.sd-jwt+json`) and Verifiable Credential types.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **SD Agent** standard defines a framework for integrating Selective Disclosure for JSON Web Tokens (SD-JWT) with Agent-to-Agent (A2A) systems. Its primary goal is to provide a privacy-preserving mechanism for autonomous agent discovery and identity management. By utilizing the **SD-Card** format—an SD-JWT encoding of traditional Agent Cards—the protocol allows agents to reveal only the specific capabilities, contact information, and operational metadata required for a given interaction context.

Key architectural components include **Agent Registries** (Issuers), **Agents** (Holders), and **Services/Clients** (Verifiers). The standard addresses critical limitations in legacy A2A implementations, such as information leakage, weak authentication, and context-blind disclosure. By incorporating **Key Binding**, it ensures that the entity presenting the SD-Card is the legitimate holder of the associated private key, thereby preventing replay attacks and ensuring cryptographic integrity.

The specification also introduces **Discovery Contexts** (e.g., public vs. internal), which dictate which claims are revealed during the discovery phase. This prevents mass enumeration of agent capabilities by unauthorized parties and supports unlinkable presentations to avoid cross-context tracking of agent activities. Furthermore, it details integration paths with the Model Context Protocol and advanced authorization flows like role-based disclosure and sub-agent delegation.

---

### Secure Intent Protocol: JWT Compatible Agentic Identity and Workflow Management (Agentic JWT)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/97d78f3a-32f8-4233-b964-59a21e22a8fc
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-goswami-agentic-jwt/
- Tags: OAuth 2.0, JWT, AI Agents, Identity, Zero-Trust, Security, Agentic AI
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An extension to OAuth 2.0 and JWT designed to address authorization challenges unique to autonomous agentic AI systems. It introduces cryptographic agent identity through checksums and workflow-aware token binding to ensure alignment between user intent and autonomous agent actions.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Abhishek Goswami (Individual) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/abhishek@abhishekgoswami.io)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)

- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Internet-Draft (Status Page)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-goswami-agentic-jwt/) (Primary Spec)
- [Draft Text (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-goswami-agentic-jwt-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [Draft HTML](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-goswami-agentic-jwt-00.html) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The protocol is currently in its first iteration (**draft-00**), released on **December 31, 2024**. Recent updates focus on defining the core architectural components for agentic identity. 

Most notably, the draft introduces the **`agent_checksum` grant type**, which allows an Authorization Server to validate an agent's "DNA"—consisting of its system prompts, available tools, and underlying code—before issuing a token. The update also includes a detailed **sequence hash computation** method to link multi-agent delegation chains, ensuring that when a supervisor agent delegates a task to a sub-agent, the cryptographic "intent" remains intact and verifiable across the execution flow. Security analysis has been expanded to include a **threat matrix** specifically targeting AI-related vulnerabilities like prompt injection used for privilege escalation.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Secure Intent Protocol (Agentic JWT)** is an extension to the OAuth 2.0 framework designed to address specific security risks posed by autonomous AI agents. Traditional authorization models assume a deterministic relationship between a user's intent and a client application's actions. However, in agentic AI systems, a "non-deterministic" model may generate sub-agents or dynamic workflows that deviate from the original user intent—a phenomenon termed the **"intent-execution separation problem."**

This protocol introduces a "Zero-Trust" architecture for AI agents by requiring cryptographic verification of an agent's identity and its specific workflow. Key components include **Agent Checksums**, which are unique identifiers derived from an agent's system prompts, toolsets, and configurations. By binding these checksums to the authorization process, the protocol prevents unauthorized agents from assuming the privileges of a human user or other high-privileged agents. 

Furthermore, the standard introduces a new **OAuth 2.0 grant type (`agent_checksum`)** and mechanisms for workflow-aware token binding. This ensures that a token issued to an agent is valid only for the specific, pre-authorized steps of an AI-driven task. It also incorporates a version of **Proof-of-Possession (PoP)** at the agent level to mitigate the risk of token replay by separate malicious agents running within the same multi-agent environment. The specification maintains backward compatibility with existing OAuth 2.0 infrastructure while providing the granularity needed for modern AI orchestration.

---

### Security Requirements for AI Agents (A2A)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/9b98570e-fcd8-44fa-bbd0-32515e9968f1
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ni-a2a-ai-agent-security-requirements/
- Tags: AI Agents, Security Requirements, Agent-to-Agent, Identity, Access Control
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This document specifies security requirements for AI agents across various interaction stages including provisioning, registration, discovery, and cross-domain interconnection. It focuses on establishing a secure framework for agent-to-agent (A2A) communication and access control.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Ni Yuan (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/niyuan1@huawei.com)
- Peter Chunchi Liu (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/liuchunchi@huawei.com)
- Qiangzhou Gao (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/gaoqiangzhou@huawei.com)
- Zhenbin Li (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/lizhenbin@huawei.com)

- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)

- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Internet-Draft (Text)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ni-a2a-ai-agent-security-requirements-01.txt) (Documentation)
- [Internet-Draft (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ni-a2a-ai-agent-security-requirements-01.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ni-a2a-ai-agent-security-requirements/history/) (Other)
- [Email Expansions](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ni-a2a-ai-agent-security-requirements/email/) (Mailing List)

**What's New:**

The latest update (version 01, published February 2026) expands on the specialized components for agent security. It introduces the concepts of the **Agent Credential Authority (ACA)** and **Agent Registry Service (ARS)**. Notably, it adds a requirement for an **AI Bill of Materials (AIBOM)** during the credential request phase to ensure the integrity of the agent’s supply chain, including models and datasets. The update also explicitly addresses the risk of **Prompt Injection**, mandating that agents use secure intermediaries or platform-level secret managers rather than holding direct access to secrets. Furthermore, it strengthens the alignment with the **WIMSE** (Workload Identity in Multi-Service Environments) initiative for AI agent identity mapping.

**Discussion Summary:**

This informational Internet-Draft outlines the security architecture and requirements for autonomous AI agents, specifically focusing on multi-agent systems and cross-domain interactions. As AI agents increasingly handle sensitive tasks across various business scenarios, the document identifies critical security gaps in current deployments. The proposed architecture introduces four primary components: a **Firewall** for traffic control, a **Master Agent** for central orchestration and policy management, individual **Agents**, and **Heterogeneous Systems** (databases, APIs, etc.).

The draft categorizes security requirements into three main sequential interaction stages:
1.  **Provisioning, Registration, and Discovery:** Establishing initial trust through hardware roots of trust (TPM/HSM) or manufacturer credentials (IDevID). It introduces the **Agent Credential Authority (ACA)** for issuing X.509 or token-based identities and the **Agent Registry Service (ARS)** for discovery. A key requirement includes the use of an **AI Bill of Materials (AIBOM)** to inventory models and datasets.
2.  **Cross-domain Interconnection:** Focusing on secure transmission and authenticated external calls between different trust domains.
3.  **Access Control:** Implementing zero-trust principles where the Master Agent validates access tokens and manages credentials for heterogeneous internal systems.

The document emphasizes that AI agents should not have direct access to long-term secrets due to prompt injection risks; instead, they should utilize platform-level secret managers and temporary access tokens. It also aligns with existing IETF work, specifically the **WIMSE** (Workload Identity in Multi-Service Environments) group, for identity management.

---

### Semantic Conventions for GenAI agent and framework spans (OTEL)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/37b22337-7024-4fd6-87bc-f00d35a8a18b
- Organization: OpenTelemetry
- Specification: https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/gen-ai/gen-ai-agent-spans/
- Tags: GenAI, Observability, Telemetry, Semantic Conventions, Tracing, LLM, Agents
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Defines a standardized set of telemetry attributes for Generative AI applications, specifically focusing on agent and framework-level spans. It provides a transition plan for instrumentations to opt-in to experimental GenAI conventions while maintaining backwards compatibility.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- OpenTelemetry Maintainers Building GenAI Semantic Conventions (OpenTelemetry) — Author

- Riccardo Magliocchetti (Elastic) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/xrmx)
- Vladimir Mihailenco (uptrace) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vmihailenco)
- my-git9 (DaoCloud) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/my-git9)
- badhon — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/badhon495)
- Abraham  — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/abalso0)
- Clément Duveau (Grafana Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/clementduveau)
- Ihor Sychevskyi (phalcon) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Arhell)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Semantic Conventions for GenAI agent and framework spans (Primary Spec)](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/gen-ai/gen-ai-agent-spans/) (Primary Spec)
- [OpenTelemetry Registry](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/registry/) (Documentation)
- [OpenTelemetry Specification GitHub](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-specification) (Github)

**What's New:**

The most significant recent update is the implementation of the **Stability Transition Plan** for GenAI conventions. This introduces the `OTEL_SEMCONV_STABILITY_OPT_IN` environment variable with the value `gen_ai_latest_experimental`. This allows developers to test the latest experimental span and attribute definitions without forcing breaking changes on users of older (v1.36.0 or prior) instrumentations. The specification has also been integrated into the **OpenTelemetry Registry**, making its attributes and entities automatically discoverable within the broader OTel ecosystem. Current work focuses on refining span names and units of measure before the conventions move toward a stable release.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Semantic Conventions for GenAI agent and framework spans** is an OpenTelemetry specification currently in the **Development** phase. It defines a standardized set of attributes, metric names, and span structures for observability in Generative AI applications, specifically targeting agents and underlying frameworks. 

The standard aims to provide a unified way to instrument GenAI workflows, allowing developers to track the lifecycle of agentic operations, including tool calls, reasoning steps, and model interactions. A critical component of this standard is the **stability transition plan**. To avoid breaking changes in existing instrumentations, OpenTelemetry has introduced a specific opt-in mechanism. Instrumentations using versions prior to **v1.36.0** are instructed to maintain their current output by default. Developers wishing to utilize the newest experimental GenAI conventions must explicitly opt-in using the environment variable `OTEL_SEMCONV_STABILITY_OPT_IN` set to `gen_ai_latest_experimental`.

Key topics covered by the conventions include:
- **Span Definitions:** Identifying specific operations like agent tasks, tool executions, and prompt processing.
- **Attributes:** Standardized metadata for tracking model versions, token counts, and execution context.
- **Registry Integration:** These conventions are automatically integrated into the OpenTelemetry Registry for global discoverability.
- **Experimental Status:** As the spec is in active development, users are cautioned that breaking changes may occur between experimental versions until a stable release is declared.

---

### Structured and Constraint Extensions for OAuth Scopes (OAuth Agent Scopes)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/d48d5d5f-614c-4665-986e-9caff90a31c2
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-chen-oauth-scope-agent-extensions
- Tags: OAuth 2.0, AI Agents, Authorization, Security, Scopes, Fine-grained Access Control
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Defines a structured format for OAuth 2.0 scopes to support fine-grained permissions for AI Agent skills and modular capability units. It introduces a colon-separated syntax for specifying resource types, actions, targets, and constraints.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (HTML)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-chen-oauth-scope-agent-extensions-00) (Primary Spec)
- [Plain Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-chen-oauth-scope-agent-extensions-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [XML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-chen-oauth-scope-agent-extensions-00.xml) (Documentation)
- [IETF Datatracker Page](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-chen-oauth-scope-agent-extensions/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The version **-00** draft was recently published, introducing the `[resource_type]:[action]:[target][:constraints]` syntax. Key updates include:
- **Defined Resource Types**: Introduces standard identifiers for `fs` (file system), `cmd` (command execution), `net` (network), `tool` (API invocation), and `scheduler`.
- **Structured Scope Negotiation**: Outlines a modified OAuth flow where the Authorization Server (AS) fetches module metadata to tailor the requested scopes before presenting them to the user.
- **Support for Constraints**: Adds an optional `:constraints` field to scope tokens to allow for key-value pairs that further restrict permissions.
- **Forward Compatibility**: Explicitly includes a `:reserve` segment for future extensions and mandates that implementations handle unknown actions gracefully.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Structured and Constraint Extensions for OAuth Scopes** is an Internet-Draft aimed at addressing the limitations of simple, space-delimited OAuth 2.0 scope strings, particularly within AI Agent ecosystems. As AI Agents increasingly use "Modular Capability Units" (or "Skills"), there is a growing need for a standardized, machine-readable way to request and grant fine-grained permissions for specific operations like file system access, network requests, and command-line execution.

The core of the proposal is a colon-separated syntax for scope values: `[resource_type]:[action]:[target][:constraints]`. This structure allows for precise authorization. For example, instead of a broad "read" permission, a client can request `fs:read:/docs/report.pdf`, where `fs` is the resource type, `read` is the action, and the file path is the target. The draft also introduces the concept of "constraints" to further limit the scope (e.g., time-bound access or rate limits).

The specification maps these interactions to standard OAuth 2.0 roles. The AI Agent acts as the **Client**, the end-user as the **Resource Owner**, and a trusted entity (often the agent platform) as the **Authorization Server**. The **Resource Server** is split into two roles: a management server for module installation and a user resource server for controlling access to local tools and data during execution. By formalizing this structure, the draft aims to enhance security and user control over third-party modular skills while maintaining backward compatibility with existing OAuth token flows. Currently, the draft is an individual submission and has not yet been adopted by a formal IETF working group.

---

### System for Cross-domain Identity Management: Agentic Identity Schema (SCIM-AGENT-SCHEMA)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/2e27898a-7ae7-4636-bad2-97fc20c67f78
- Organization: IETF Project
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-wahl-scim-agent-schema/
- Tags: AI Agents, Identity Management, SCIM, JSON Schema, Authentication
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Provides a platform-neutral schema for representing AI agents' identities in JSON format within the System for Cross-domain Identity Management (SCIM). It enables agentic identities to be transferred, authenticated, and authorized to interact with services.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Mark Wahl (Microsoft) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/mwahl@microsoft.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)

- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)

- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)

- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)

- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Plain Text Specification](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-wahl-scim-agent-schema-01.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Specification](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-wahl-scim-agent-schema-01.html) (Documentation)
- [XML Specification](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-wahl-scim-agent-schema-01.xml) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-wahl-scim-agent-schema/history/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The most significant update in the **-01 iteration (August 2025)** is the refinement of the `AgenticIdentity` resource schema. Key changes include the formalization of the `agenticApplicationId` for multi-identity correlation and the detailed structure of the `oAuthClientIdentifiers` complex attribute. This version specifically addresses how agents authenticate via **OAuth token exchange**, mapping sub-attributes like `issuer`, `subject`, and `audiences` directly to standard JWT claims. Furthermore, the draft now explicitly defines updates to the existing SCIM Group schema, allowing the `members` attribute to natively support the `AgenticIdentity` type, ensuring that AI agents can be managed within standard enterprise group structures.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **System for Cross-domain Identity Management: Agentic Identity Schema** is an IETF Internet-Draft that extends the base SCIM specifications (RFC 7644 and RFC 7643) to accommodate the unique identity requirements of AI agents. As AI agents increasingly interact with cloud services, there is a growing need to provision, manage, and authorize these non-human entities using standardized protocols. 

The core of this standard is the introduction of the `AgenticIdentity` resource type (`urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:AgenticIdentity`). Unlike traditional human user schemas, this schema focuses on attributes relevant to automated entities, such as `agenticApplicationId` for correlating multiple identities to a single application and `oAuthClientIdentifiers` to facilitate authentication via OAuth token exchange (RFC 8693). It also maintains parity with standard SCIM lifecycle controls, allowing agents to be assigned to groups, roles, and entitlements. 

Key technical decisions in the draft include:
*   **Schema Extension:** Augmenting the standard SCIM "members" attribute to explicitly allow references to `AgenticIdentity` resources.
*   **Ownership:** The inclusion of an `owners` attribute to map agents to responsible parties (users or groups).
*   **Interoperability:** Alignment with existing OAuth claims (e.g., `aud`, `iss`, `sub`) within the agent's identity record.

The specification is currently in the "Informational" track and is being discussed within the SCIM Working Group. Open areas typically include refined security considerations regarding agent impersonation and the specific mechanics of delegating access rights from human owners to automated agents.

---

### The agent:// Protocol -- A URI-Based Framework for Interoperable Agents (agent://)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/f6149ce7-9098-4563-8fda-4330d1f0a549
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-narvaneni-agent-uri/02/
- Tags: AI Agents, URI, Interoperability, Protocol, Orchestration
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A URI template-based framework for addressing, invoking, and interoperating with autonomous and semi-autonomous software agents. It provides a layered architecture for capability discovery, contracts, and orchestration across different agent ecosystems.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Yaswanth Narvaneni (Individual) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/yaswanth@gmail.com)
- IvoryHeart — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/IvoryHeart)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)

- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)

- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)

- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Draft source on GitHub](https://github.com/agent-uri/agent-uri/tree/main/docs/rfc) (Github)
- [Reference implementation, discussion and development](https://github.com/agent-uri/agent-uri/) (Reference Impl)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-narvaneni-agent-uri/history/) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The most significant recent update is the release of **draft-narvaneni-agent-uri-02** in November 2024. This version includes refined definitions for the URI components and expanded sections on the security and privacy implications of globally addressable agents. The development team has also consolidated community discussions on the project's GitHub repository, focusing on creating a reference implementation and improving the documentation for the `agent://` resolution process. There is a growing focus on aligning the protocol with existing identification standards to ensure it does not duplicate efforts in the decentralized identity space.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **agent:// protocol** is a proposed URI-based framework designed to provide a standardized, interoperable method for identifying and interacting with autonomous agents across different platforms and environments. Developed as an Internet-Draft within the IETF, the standard aims to solve the fragmentation in agent-to-agent and human-to-agent communication by defining a consistent addressing scheme.

The protocol structure follows the standard URI syntax (RFC 3986), utilizing the `agent:` scheme. It is designed to include components for identifying the agent's authority (host or platform), its specific identity (path), and optional parameters for capabilities or session-specific instructions through queries and fragments. By providing a uniform way to "address" an agent, the framework facilitates discovery, authentication, and the invocation of agentic functions regardless of the underlying implementation (e.g., Large Language Model backends, specialized robotic controllers, or software bots).

Key objectives of the framework include:
- **Interoperability:** Allowing agents from different developers or platforms to reference and communicate with each other.
- **Portability:** Enabling agent identities to remain consistent even if moved between hosting environments.
- **Extensibility:** Supporting various communication protocols (like WebSockets, HTTP, or decentralized protocols) through the resolution mechanism of the URI.

The standard is currently being developed through community feedback on GitHub and formal updates to the IETF Datatracker. Open questions often revolve around security considerations, such as how to prevent agent "spoofing" and how the URI integrates with existing Distributed Identifier (DID) standards.

---

### Transaction Tokens For Agents (OTTA)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/448f9713-fef5-4ffe-bc9d-21d19445bbf4
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents/
- Tags: OAuth, Security, AI AgentsContext Propagation, Transaction Tokens, Authentication
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An extension to the OAuth Transaction Tokens framework to support agent context propagation. It defines 'actor' and 'principal' fields to allow services to distinguish between the agent performing an action and the entity that initiated it.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Ashay Raut (Amazon) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/asharaut@amazon.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)

- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)

- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)

- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)
