- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Interactive HTML version](https://doc.html/draft-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents-04) (Documentation)
- [Text version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents-04.txt) (Documentation)
- [XML source](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents-04.xml) (Documentation)
- [BibXML](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/bibxml3/draft-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents-04.xml) (Documentation)
- [Email Expansions](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-oauth-transaction-tokens-for-agents/email/) (Mailing List)

**What's New:**

The latest update (**draft-04**, February 2026) refines the protocol for how Transaction Token Services (TTS) extract and map identities from incoming OAuth 2.0 access tokens. Key recent additions include:

- **Explicit mapping rules**: Instructions for the TTS to use the `aud` claim from an access token to populate the `sub` claim of the Txn-Token, and the `clientId` (or `actor`) to populate the agent's context.
- **Autonomous Flow refinement**: Clarification that autonomous agents should use the OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials Grant, with the `sub` field from the access token's `actor` claim mapping to the Txn-Token `actor` context.
- **Agentic Context**: The introduction of the `agentic_ctx` claim, a JSON object intended to carry attributes relative to an agent's specific operational constraints for policy evaluation.

**Discussion Summary:**

This standard specifies an extension to the **OAuth Transaction Tokens (Txn-Tokens)** framework to support context propagation for AI agents. As AI agents often operate with a degree of autonomy or across complex service graphs, traditional authorization models struggle to track whether an action was initiated by a human or the agent itself.

The draft introduces two primary context fields to the Txn-Token:
- **actor**: Identifies the AI agent performing the action.
- **principal**: Identifies the human or system entity that initiated the agent's task.

The framework supports two distinct operational modes:
1. **Principal-Initiated Flow**: Where a human invokes an agent, and the token carries both the agent's identity (as the actor) and the human's identity (as the principal).
2. **Autonomous Flow**: Where an agent operates independently (e.g., on a schedule). In this case, the 'principal' field can be omitted, and the agent's identity is derived from the OAuth client credentials.

By embedding this metadata within short-lived, signed JWTs, the standard allows downstream services in a distributed system to perform granular access control and maintain a clear audit trail of "who" (agent) is acting for "whom" (human user). additionally, it introduces an optional `agentic_ctx` claim for conveying specific operational constraints of the agent.

---

### Universal Tool Calling Protocol (UTCP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/d9494392-fa34-4043-be01-6511f9886e84
- Organization: UTCP Foundation
- Specification: https://utcp.io/about/RFC
- Tags: tool calling, AI agents, interoperability吹, API discovery吹, decentralized
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A decentralized protocol that enables AI agents to discover and use external tools by interacting with them directly via their native protocols. It eliminates the 'wrapper tax' by using a standardized 'Manual' (JSON file) to describe tools and their transport requirements without requiring changes to the underlying APIs.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Razvan-Ion Radulescu (UTCP) — Author
- actions-user (actions) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/actions-user)
- Razvan Radulescu — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/h3xxit)
- Juan V. (universal-tool-calling-protocol) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/edujuan)
- zero — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bruce-gene)
- perrozzi — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/perrozzi)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Specification Feed (RSS)](https://utcp.io/blog/rss.xml) (Mailing List)
- [GitHub Organization](https://github.com/universal-tool-calling-protocol) (Github)
- [Specification Issues](https://github.com/universal-tool-calling-protocol/utcp-specification/issues/18) (Github)
- [Documentation](https://utcp.io/) (Documentation)
- [Discord Server](https://discord.gg/ZpMbQ8jRbD) (Discord)

**What's New:**

The UTCP project recently published a detailed technical critique titled "Why MCP Servers Are a Nightmare for Engineers," highlighting issues with statefulness, STDIO transports, and security in the Model Context Protocol. This aligns with the recent release of **UTCP v1.1** documentation. The protocol is now actively promoting its **RFC for direct API interaction**, emphasizing a model that removes the middle-man server requirement. Recent updates focus on providing a "Direct Alternative" to MCP, with a specific focus on reducing latency and avoiding "Prompt Bloat" by moving away from injecting massive tool definitions into LLM context windows. Language implementations for **Python, TypeScript, and Go** are currently available in the project's GitHub organization.

**Discussion Summary:**

The Universal Tool Calling Protocol (UTCP) is a specialized standard designed to facilitate direct, scalable, and lightweight interactions between Large Language Models (LLMs) and various tools or APIs. Positioned as a direct alternative to Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP), UTCP prioritizes a "stateless" architecture that mimics traditional developer workflows with REST or gRPC endpoints. This approach aims to eliminate the need for persistent "side-car" server processes and complex middle-man infrastructure often required by MCP for simple tasks.

The protocol addresses several identified "pain points" in the AI tool-calling ecosystem, specifically targeting the overhead of stateful session management, ambiguous transport definitions (such as the confusion surrounding STDIO-based child processes), and the lack of native security standards in existing protocols. UTCP focuses on "Zero Latency Overhead" by allowing AI agents to call existing endpoints directly. It also seeks to solve the "Prompt Bloat" problem where clients jam hundreds of tool definitions into an LLM's context window, proposing a more efficient discovery and routing mechanism.

UTCP's design philosophy is centered on ease of enterprise adoption, enabling teams to expose their existing, battle-tested HTTP APIs to AI models without re-implementing them behind stateful wrappers. While acknowledging that MCP excels in rich context packaging and server-initiated LLM calls for complex IDE integrations, UTCP carves out its space as the efficient choice for standardized, direct tool execution. The project maintains implementations in Python, TypeScript, and Go.

---

### WIMSE Applicability for AI Agents (WIMSE AI Agent Identity)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/9f216bbc-9212-43e4-95f3-7c7c06064a5f
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ni-wimse-ai-agent-identity/
- Tags: AI Agents, Identity, WIMSE, Security, Credentials, Agentic AI
- Status: Draft
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This specification discusses the applicability of Workload Identity in Multi-Service Environments (WIMSE) to Agentic AI. It aims to establish independent identities and credential management mechanisms for AI agents.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Ni Yuan (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/niyuan1@huawei.com)
- Peter Chunchi Liu (Huawei) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/liuchunchi@huawei.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)

- Russ Housley — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/russhousley)

- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)

- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Current Draft (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ni-wimse-ai-agent-identity-02.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ni-wimse-ai-agent-identity/history/) (Other)
- [Email Expansions](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ni-wimse-ai-agent-identity/email/) (Mailing List)
- [WIMSE Working Group (Related)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/wg/wimse/about/) (Working Group)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ni-wimse-ai-agent-identity/history/) (Other)
- [Plain Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ni-wimse-ai-agent-identity-02.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ni-wimse-ai-agent-identity-02.html) (Documentation)
- [IETF WIMSE Working Group](https://datatracker.ietf.org/group/wimse/about/) (Working Group)

**What's New:**

The latest update, version **-02** (released March 2025), further refines the discussion on how AI agents should be treated as independent workloads within the WIMSE framework. This version focuses on clarifying the "independent identities" requirement, ensuring that credentials managed for AI agents can survive cross-system hops while maintaining a link to the original execution context. The authors, Yuan Ni and Chunchi Peter Liu, have updated the document to better align with the core WIMSE Working Group's emerging patterns for workload identity. Recent activity has shifted toward defining concrete credential management mechanisms specifically for "Agentic AI" scenarios.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **WIMSE Applicability for AI Agents** standard (codified in `draft-ni-wimse-ai-agent-identity`) focuses on extending the Workload Identity in Multi-System Environments (WIMSE) framework to the specific needs of **Agentic AI**. As AI agents increasingly operate autonomously across diverse systems, they require independent identities and robust credential management mechanisms distinct from the human users who may have initiated them.

The specification aims to define how AI agents can be uniquely identified, how they acquire security credentials, and how these credentials are used to authenticate and authorize their actions across multi-system environments. Key topics include:
*   **Independent Identity:** Establishing a clear identity for AI agents to ensure non-repudiation and fine-grained access control.
*   **Credential Management:** Mechanims for issuing, renewing, and revoking credentials specifically for autonomous workloads.
*   **WIMSE Alignment:** Ensuring that AI agent identity patterns are compatible with broader WIMSE architectures, which traditionally handle microservices and remote workloads.

By providing a standardized way to handle AI agent identities, the draft seeks to mitigate security risks associated with autonomous AI actions and ensure interoperability between different AI platforms and the services they interact with.

---

## Emerging Standards

### Agent Commerce Kit (ACK)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/22430209-6944-43e1-be8b-88205543a631
- Organization: Catena Labs
- Specification: https://www.agentcommercekit.com/
- Tags: Protocol, Agents, Security, Payments
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Agent Commerce Kit (ACK) is an open-source toolkit that enables AI agents to securely participate in commerce through two protocols: ACK-ID for verifiable agent identity using W3C DIDs and VCs, and ACK-Pay for standardized agent-native payments across diverse settlement rails.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Catena Labs (Catena Labs) — Author [profile](https://catenalabs.com/)
- Matt Venables (Catena Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/venables)
- Dominic Bosco — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/domleboss97)
- Ed Spencer — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/edspencer)
- Jonas Hahn (Solana Foundation) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Woody4618)

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/agentcommercekit/ack) (Github)
- [Discord Server](https://discord.gg/FfWBmaSRmt) (Discord)
- [Catena Labs Blog](https://catenalabs.com/blog) (Blog)
- [Documentation](https://www.agentcommercekit.com/overview/introduction) (Documentation)
- [Quickstart Guide](https://www.agentcommercekit.com/demos/quickstart) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The **Agent Commerce Kit (ACK)** has recently expanded its developer resources with high-quality documentation and a growing GitHub presence. Recent activity focuses on solidifying the "Agentic Commerce" category, with **Catena Labs** positioning ACK as the primary infrastructure for AI-native payments and identity. The community has seen a surge in engagement, now exceeding 11,000 members on Discord. Technical updates include the deployment of an AI-native financial infrastructure framework and refined documentation to help developers transition agents from simple reasoning tasks to full economic transactions. Efforts are currently directed at improving interoperability between autonomous agents and decentralized financial protocols.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Agent Commerce Kit (ACK)** is an open-source development framework designed to enable the "Agent Economy" by providing AI-native financial and commerce infrastructure. Developed by **Catena Labs**, the kit addresses a critical gap in the current AI landscape: while autonomous agents can reason and plan, they often lack the "real-world" capabilities to own assets, verify their identity, and execute financial transactions independently.

ACK provides a standardized set of tools and protocols that allow developers to integrate commerce capabilities into AI agents. Key features of the kit include:
- **Agentic Payments**: Infrastructure for agents to send, receive, and hold funds securely.
- **AI-Native Identity**: Systems for verifying the legitimacy of agents and link them to responsible human or corporate entities.
- **Financial Compliance**: Built-in mechanisms to handle KYC/AML requirements in an automated, agent-friendly manner.
- **Interoperability**: A common interface that allows different agentic systems to interact with traditional and decentralized commerce platforms.

The project is maintained by Catena Labs, which also cultivates the "Catena Community," a large ecosystem of over 11,000 members on Discord. By providing these foundational primitives, ACK seeks to move AI from mere "chatbots" to autonomous economic actors capable of purchasing services, managing budgets, and participating in supply chains. The kit is built with a focus on developer experience, utilizing modern web and AI tooling to ensure quick integration into existing agent frameworks.

---

### Agent Skills Specification (Skills)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/83aa1a86-38ec-45d7-8610-a28e77b6aea0
- Organization: Agent Skills
- Specification: https://agentskills.io/specification
- Tags: ai agents, autonomous agents, skills, interoperability, specification
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A format specification for defining and packaging modular capabilities, or skills, for AI agents to enable interoperability across different platforms.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Agent Skills Contributors (Agent Skills) — Author
- Jonathan Hefner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jonathanhefner)
- Eric Harmeling (Anthropic) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ericharmeling)
- Keith Lazuka (Anthropic) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/klazuka)
- Mahesh Murag — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maheshmurag)

- pandora — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pandora-s-git)

- bl-ue — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bl-ue)
- Vinh Nguyen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vinhnx)
- Umesh Unnikrishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sfc-gh-uunnikrishnan)
- Skill Creator  — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/skillcreatorai)
- Pushpak Chhajed (laravel) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pushpak1300)
- Nnenna Ndukwe (Qodo) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nnennandukwe)
- Michael Suchacz (Coder) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ibetitsmike)
- Koichi ITO (ESM, Inc. (@esminc)) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/koic)
- Kevin Tang (ex Two Sigma, Honey, American Express Software Engineer) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kevo1ution)
- Jamie Chicago — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jamiechicago312)
- Irina Chichikova (JetBrains) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/preigile)
- Igor Costa (Autohand AI) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/igorcosta)
- Harald Kirschner (Microsoft) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/digitarald)
- Duncan Ogilvie — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mrexodia)
- Dmitry Lyalin (Google | @google-gemini) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/LyalinDotCom)
- Christian Tzolov (spring-io) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/tzolov)
- Bruno Bergher (Roo Code) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/brunobergher)
- Arun Nair — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nairteashop)
- Arne Strickmann — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/arnestrickmann)
- Amber Shen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ambershen)
- Ahmad Awais (Founder & CEO @CommandCodeAI, VP DevTools @Google gDAB GDE) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ahmadawais)

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/agentskills/agentskills) (Github)
- [Agent Skills Home](https://agentskills.io/) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

Recent activity around the Agent Skills Specification has focused on enhancing the developer experience through the launch of **agentskills.io**, a dedicated documentation hub powered by Mintlify. This new site provides a centralized "Overview" and structured guides to help developers implement the open format. 

On the technical front, there have been recent updates to the GitHub repository involving the integration of **Mintlify assets** and improvements to the repository's metadata. The project is increasingly emphasizing "portable expertise," moving beyond simple tool-calling to include more complex logic packages. Development discussions have recently centered on stabilizing the core schema to ensure that skills created today remain compatible with future iterations of agentic workflows and diverse Large Language Models (LLMs).

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Agent Skills Specification** is an open-source standard designed to provide a universal format for extending the capabilities and expertise of AI agents. It addresses the fragmentation in the AI agent ecosystem by defining a common structure for "Skills"—reusable packages of logic, tools, or knowledge that can be seamlessly integrated into various agentic frameworks. The core goal of the specification is to enable interoperability, allowing developers to build a skill once and deploy it across different agent platforms without significant refactoring.

The specification typically covers structural requirements such as:
- **Skill Discovery**: How agents can find and identify available capabilities.
- **Interface Definitions**: Standardized ways of defining inputs, outputs, and execution logic (often leveraging JSON schemas or similar formats).
- **Execution Context**: Parameters and environmental data required for a skill to perform its function.
- **Portability**: Ensuring the skill remains functional regardless of the underlying LLM or orchestration layer (e.g., LangChain, AutoGen, or custom implementations).

By providing this abstraction layer, the Agent Skills Specification aims to create a "Skill Registry" or marketplace effect where specialized expertise—ranging from web browsing and file manipulation to complex financial analysis—can be modularly added to any compliant agent.

---

### agents.json (agents.json)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/6018424b-7ebf-4193-8d97-3a7febb5b2a3
- Organization: Wildcard
- Specification: https://docs.wild-card.ai/agentsjson/introduction
- Tags: API, Agents, JSON Specification, OpenAPI Extension, Agentic Interoperability
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Agents.json is an open-source JSON specification built on top of the OpenAPI standard that formally describes contracts for API and AI agent interactions. It provides a structured way for agents to understand and interact with diverse service capabilities.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Wildcard Team (Wildcard) — Author
- Kaushik Mahorker — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kmahorker)
- Yagnya Patel — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/yagnyaPatel)
- Wildcard AI — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wild-card-ai)

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/wild-card-ai/agents-json) (Github)
- [Support Email](mailto:info@wild-card.ai) (Mailing List)
- [Documentation Introduction](https://docs.wild-card.ai/agentsjson/introduction) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The standard has recently gained momentum with the inclusion of specific fields for the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)**, allowing agents to expose their tools and resources more effectively. Recent updates also include a refined 'permissions' object to clarify what actions an external agent is allowed to take. Furthermore, a community-driven list of 'Authorized Agents' is being curated to help prevent malicious crawlers from spoofing the standard. The repository has seen increased activity in the 'examples' directory, providing templates for common platforms like Shopify and Zendesk.

**Discussion Summary:**

`agents.json` is a proposed standard for AI agent discovery and interoperability, modeled after the successful `well-known` file patterns like `robots.txt` or `security.txt`. Developed by Wild Card AI, the standard provides a structured way for websites to declare the presence, capabilities, and contact information of their AI agents. This allows external systems and other AI agents to programmatically discover how to interact with a specific domain's automated representatives. 

The specification defines a JSON-based schema that includes fields for the agent's name, description, API endpoints, supported protocols (such as Model Context Protocol - MCP), and authentication requirements. By hosting this file at a predictable location (typically `/.well-known/agents.json`), service providers can ensure their agents are "indexable" and "crawlable" by the broader AI ecosystem, fostering a more connected and automated web. The project is currently hosted on GitHub and is seeking community feedback to refine the schema for various use cases, including customer support bots, trading agents, and data retrievers.

---

### AGNTCY Identity Specification (AGNTCY)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/7c14b85a-1ceb-4568-a5f0-aebd76d3276c
- Organization: AGNTCY
- Specification: https://github.com/agntcy/identity-spec
- Tags: identity, agents, MCP, multi-agent systems破, verification破, AI agents破], title:
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A specification for onboarding, creating, and verifying identities for AI Agents, Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, and Multi-Agent Systems (MASs). It establishes a standard framework for identity management in autonomous agent ecosystems.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- AGNTCY Team (AGNTCY) — Author
- Jean DIACONU (Cisco) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jadiaconu)
- Ben Ryder (Cisco) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/beryder)
- Rafael Silva (Cisco Systems, Inc. @cisco) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rafaelsilva29)
- Mohamed Tahar KEDJOUR (Cisco) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mkedjour)
- mayannuz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mayannuz)
- hmuyal — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/hmuyal)
- copasseron — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/copasseron)

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/agntcy/identity-spec) (Github)
- [Main Specification](https://github.com/agntcy/identity-spec/blob/main/README.md) (Primary Spec)

**What's New:**

The AGNTCY Identity Specification has recently moved into an active draft phase. The most significant recent activity includes the formalization of the **README** and initial architecture diagrams that outline the relationship between AI Agents and their Controllers. Key focus areas in the latest updates include the standardization of metadata fields for agents to declare their capabilities and the integration of DID-based authentication flows. The project is currently inviting developers from the AI and decentralized identity (DID) communities to provide feedback on the interoperability of the proposed credential formats.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **AGNTCY Identity Specification** is an open standard designed to provide a secure, decentralized identity framework specifically tailored for AI agents. As autonomous agents increasingly interact across different platforms and with human users, the specification addresses the critical need for a verifiable way to establish agent identity, ownership, and permissions without relying on central authorities.

The standard leverages decentralized identifiers (DIDs) and verifiable credentials to ensure that an agent's actions can be cryptographically linked to its controller. Key topics covered by the specification include:
- **Agent Self-Sovereignty**: Enabling agents to hold their own identifiers and present proofs of identity.
- **Controller Linking**: Defining how a human or organizational owner can prove authority over an AI agent.
- **Interoperability**: Ensuring that identities are recognizable across different agentic frameworks and autonomous ecosystems.
- **Security Protocols**: Establishing best practices for key management and rotation for automated systems.

By providing a structured markdown-based specification on GitHub, the project encourages community collaboration to build a trust layer for the "Agentic Web." This allows developers to build agents that can be trusted to perform financial transactions, access private data, or interact with other agents securely.

---

### AI Agent Authentication and Authorization (AI-AGENT-AUTH)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/e31a4ba1-488f-4a62-843b-d181c8b5e289
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-klrc-aiagent-auth/
- Tags: AI Agents, Authentication, Authorization, OAuth 2.0, WIMSE, Security
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Proposes a model for authentication and authorization of AI agent interactions by leveraging WIMSE and OAuth 2.0. It aims to provide a framework for using existing standards and identifying gaps to guide future standardization for agents.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Pieter Kasselman (Independent) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/prkasselman@gmail.com)
- Jeff Lombardo (Amazon) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/jeffsec@amazon.com)
- Yaroslav Rosomakho (Zscaler) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/yrosomakho@zscaler.com)
- Brian Campbell (Independent) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/brian.d.campbell@gmail.com)

- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Text Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-klrc-aiagent-auth-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-klrc-aiagent-auth-00.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-klrc-aiagent-auth/history/) (Other)
- [IETF Mailman Lists](https://mailman3.ietf.org/mailman3/lists/) (Mailing List)

**What's New:**

The standard is currently in its first iteration (**-00 draft**), introduced in March 2026. This initial release focuses on defining the AI Agent as a specific type of "workload" and establishes the **Agent Identity Management System (AIMS)** framework. Recent focus areas highlighted in the draft include the use of **WIMSE Proof Tokens (WPTs)** and **HTTP Message Signatures** for application-layer authentication, as well as exploring privacy considerations for cross-domain access and the discovery of Authorization Server capabilities in dynamic AI environments.

**Discussion Summary:**

This informational document proposes a comprehensive model for the authentication and authorization of AI agents, treating them as autonomous workloads. Rather than inventing new protocols, the standard aims to consolidate existing "prior art" from the identity and security landscape to prevent fragmentation in the rapidly evolving AI field. The framework defines an AI Agent as a workload that iteratively interacts with Large Language Models (LLMs), tools, and services until a termination condition is met. 

The core of the proposal is the **Agent Identity Management System (AIMS)**, a conceptual model encompassing identity functions such as unique identifiers, cryptographic credentials, and attestation. The document advocates for leveraging established standards including **OAuth 2.0** for delegated authorization, **SPIFFE** for workload identity, and **WIMSE** (Workload Identity in Multi-System Environments) for architecture. It specifically addresses challenges such as agent-to-agent communication, human-in-the-loop requirements, and the need for dynamic "Transaction Tokens" to reduce risk during complex, multi-step agent operations. By providing this mapping of existing standards to AI-specific use cases, the draft seeks to identify functional gaps that may require future targeted standardization efforts.

---

### Cross-Domain Interoperability Framework for AI Agent Collaboration (CDI)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/1cd8d0f6-bd37-43cd-8132-3248de6dde7c
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-cui-dmsc-agent-cdi/
- Tags: AI Agents, Interoperability, Federated Identity, Cross-Domain Collaboration, Multi-Agent Systems
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A framework for enabling seamless cross-domain interoperability among AI agents operating across different networks, administrative domains, and heterogeneous platforms. It specifies mechanisms for agent discovery, capability negotiation, trust delegation, and federated policy enforcement to facilitate scalable multi-domain AI collaboration.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Yong Cui (Tsinghua University) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/cuiyong@tsinghua.edu.cn)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)

- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)

- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)

- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Internet-Draft Text](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-cui-dmsc-agent-cdi-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [Internet-Draft HTML](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-cui-dmsc-agent-cdi-00.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-cui-dmsc-agent-cdi/history/) (Other)
- [IPR Disclosures](https://datatracker.ietf.org/ipr/search/?submit=draft&id=draft-cui-dmsc-agent-cdi) (Other)

**What's New:**

The standard is in its earliest stage with the publication of **version 00** in February 2026. Recent updates focus on integrating the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** to standardize how agents interact with external tools and hosts. A significant addition is the **Security Enhancement Layer (SEL)**, which introduces "Client Shields" and "Server Shields" to protect user and provider interests without requiring modifications to the underlying protocols. The draft also recently added extensive use cases spanning smart city federation, international research data commons, and global energy grid coordination to justify the multi-domain approach.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Cross-Domain Interoperability Framework for AI Agent Collaboration (CDI)** is a proposed IETF informational standard designed to enable AI agents to collaborate across different administrative domains, networks, and platforms. Traditionally, AI agents are confined to single-domain environments where trust and policies are centrally managed. This framework addresses the growing need for cross-enterprise and cross-border collaboration by providing mechanisms for identity federation, trust establishment without a central authority, and policy harmonization. 

Core architectural components of the framework include:
*   **Interoperability Gateways**: Facilitate communication and policy enforcement between domains.
*   **Agent Registries**: Enable discovery of agent capabilities.
*   **Trust Establishment Protocols**: Defining how domains can federate and delegate authority.
*   **Federated Identity Model**: Utilizing standards like Decentralized Identifiers (DID) and Verifiable Credentials (VC) to manage agent identities.
*   **Capability Discovery**: A standardized language and protocol for agents to negotiate what tasks they can perform for one another.

The framework emphasizes domain autonomy, minimal trust assumptions, and a fail-safe design to ensure that security breaches in one domain do not cascade to others. It also incorporates the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** for standardizing tool invocation and introduces a **Security Enhancement Layer (SEL)** to provide transparent security controls like data backflow protection and dual authorization.

---

### llms.txt (llms.txt)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/1b83a5a6-344d-4910-acc4-c11074236f68
- Organization: Answer.AI
- Specification: https://llmstxt.org/
- Tags: Standard
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

llms.txt is a standardized markdown file that gives LLMs concise, expert-level context and pointers to detailed docs, avoiding noisy HTML. It is human- and machine-readable in a precise format suitable for deterministic parsing (parsers/regex). Sites are encouraged to offer clean markdown versions of pages at the same URL with .md appended (or index.html.md for path URLs) to support reliable ingestion.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Jeremy Howard (Answer.AI) — Author [profile](https://x.com/jeremyphoward)
- Hamel Husain (Parlance Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/hamelsmu)
- Radek Osmulski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/radekosmulski)
- Derick Ruiz (abundantdev) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/dericksozo)
- Sarah Pan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sarahpannn)
- Nathan Cooper — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ncoop57)
- Adithya Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/krish-adi)
- August — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/hey-august)
- ChristophWeber — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ChristophWeber)
- Gabor Szarnyas (DuckDB, @LDBC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/szarnyasg)

- jjallaire — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jjallaire)

- Christophe Dervieux (https://posit.co) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/cderv)
- Gordon Woodhull (Posit PBC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gordonwoodhull)
- Mickaël Canouil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mcanouil)

- Michael Hatherly (PumasAI) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/MichaelHatherly)
- eitsupi — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/eitsupi)
- Robrecht Cannoodt (edaro) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rcannood)
- Winston Chang (posit-pbc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wch)
- gjirokastrelbasanit34 — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gjirokastrelbasanit34)
- Brian Lambert (posit-dev) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/softwarenerd)
- MBe-iUS (iUS Institut für Umwelttechnologien und Strahlenschutz GmbH) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/MBe-iUS)
- Jannik Buhr (Heidelberg University; HITS gGmbH) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jmbuhr)
- Mohammad Rizki (Faculty of Medicine Universitas Mataram) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mohammadrizki)
- Matthew Lynch (posit-dev @posit-hosted @rstudio) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mslynch)
- Theodore Ni — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/tjni)
- Salim B — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/salim-b)
- Mara Averick (Quansight) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/batpigandme)
- Jeremie Knuesel — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/knuesel)
- Gianluca Baio (University College London) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/giabaio)
- Colin Rundel (Duke University) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rundel)

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/answerdotai/llms-txt) (Github)
- [Python module & CLI documentation](https://llmstxt.org/intro.html) (Documentation)
- [JavaScript Implementation](https://llmstxt.org/llmstxt-js.html) (Reference Impl)
- [X (Twitter) Profile](https://x.com/answerdotai) (Other)
- [Sample llms.txt (FastHTML)](https://www.fastht.ml/docs/llms.txt) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

Recent updates to the `llms.txt` ecosystem focus on cross-platform parsing and practical tooling. A **JavaScript implementation** has been introduced, allowing for client-side parsing of `llms.txt` files into JSON objects. The **Python library** (version reachable via `pip install llms-txt`) now includes a CLI that can automatically fetch all links defined in a remote `llms.txt` and bundle them into an XML format optimized for LLM context windows. Additionally, established frameworks like **FastHTML** and **MonsterUI** have officially adopted the standard, providing live `llms.txt` files that document their specific APIs and "rules to remember" for AI-assisted coding.

**Discussion Summary:**

The `llms.txt` standard is a proposal for a structural markdown-based file format designed to help Large Language Models (LLMs) quickly understand the context, rules, and documentation of a project. Hosted by Answer.AI, the standard suggests placing an `llms.txt` file at the root of a website or project to provide a "handbook" for AI tools.

The format is strictly defined yet simple:
- **Header**: A single H1 tag for the project name.
- **Summary**: An optional blockquote providing a high-level overview.
- **Project Information**: A free-text area (often including bulleted lists) for specific rules, mental models, or constraints.
- **Sections**: H2 headers (like `## Docs` or `## Examples`) containing markdown lists of links to full documentation files.
- **Optional/Expanded Context**: A special `## Optional` section for secondary information that shouldn't always be included in the primary context window.

By providing this file, developers enable LLMs and AI agents (like GitHub Copilot, Cursor, or ChatGPT) to discover and ingest high-quality, relevant documentation rather than relying on outdated training data or expensive broad-crawling of a site. The standard is supported by a Python library (`llms-txt`) that can parse these files and convert the linked resources into a single consolidated XML context document suitable for prompt injection.

---

### The Intent Token: A Cryptographic Authorization Primitive for Autonomous Agents (Intent Token)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/0c97d3a2-5f9d-4caa-b174-2ce76c756686
- Organization: IETF Outer Space
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-williams-intent-token/
- Tags: AI Agents, Authorization, Cryptography, Autonomous Systems, OAuth
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

A cryptographic authorization primitive that binds autonomous agent actions to human-declared authorization envelopes. It fills the gap in existing frameworks by governing what an autonomous agent is authorized to do at the moment of action.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Jeffrey Williams (Individual) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/urls@live.com)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)

- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Internet-Draft (TXT)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-williams-intent-token-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [Internet-Draft (HTML)](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-williams-intent-token-00.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-williams-intent-token/history/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The standard was recently introduced to the IETF as an **Informational Internet-Draft (version 00)** on March 19, 2026. This initial version establishes the core JWT structure for the Intent Token, including required claims like `ibt_ver`, `principal`, `declared_intent`, and `shard`. It also introduces the concept of **SNAP-BACK enforcement**, requiring that any authorization failure results in the immediate cancellation of an agent's action before it takes effect. The draft explicitly positions itself alongside Google DeepMind's "Delegation Capability Tokens" (DCTs) as a potential standard to fill the global gap in autonomous agent governance.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **Intent Token** is a proposed cryptographic authorization primitive designed specifically for autonomous AI agents. While current standards like OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) manage identity (the "Who") and session-level access (the "What resources"), the Intent Token aims to bridge the "authorization gap" by defining what an agent is specifically authorized to **do** at the moment of action. 

The standard introduces a signed, time-bounded JSON Web Token (JWT) structure that binds an agent's intended action to a human-declared authorization envelope. This mechanism is intended to prevent "intent blindness"—where an agent interprets its scope in unintended ways—and addresses the "confused deputy" problem where agents might be manipulated by adversarial prompts. Key features include:
*   **Action Binding:** Verification of specific actions (e.g., financial limits, permitted instruments) before execution.
*   **Temporal Sharding:** Tokens are bound to short-lived "shards" (default 512 seconds), requiring frequent re-validation.
*   **Delegation Chains:** A cryptographic mechanism to ensure sub-agents cannot exceed the authorization scope of the primary agent.
*   **SNAP-BACK:** A mandatory enforcement rule that cancels any action failing validation before it has physical or market effects.

The Intent Token is part of the broader **Intent Bound Authorization (IBA)** framework and is designed to be model-agnostic and transport-agnostic, allowing it to compose with existing OAuth infrastructure.

---

### Using LISP as a Network Substrate for AI Agent Communication (LISP-AI-Agent)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/d1290eeb-137e-473e-941b-8dc510c4aa86
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-wang-lisp-ai-agent/
- Tags: Networking, AI Agents Distributed AI, LISP, Protocol
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

Explores how the Locator/ID Separation Protocol (LISP) can serve as a network substrate for distributed AI agents across cloud, edge, and endpoint environments. It outlines extensions to LISP to support context-aware mapping and AI agent-centric communication.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Wei Wang (China Telecom) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/wangw36@chinatelecom.cn)
- Chongfeng Xie (China Telecom) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/xiechf@chinatelecom.cn)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)

- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)
- Sangho Na — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/microamp)
- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)

- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)

- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Datatracker Status Page](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-wang-lisp-ai-agent/) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-wang-lisp-ai-agent/history/) (Other)
- [Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-wang-lisp-ai-agent-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-wang-lisp-ai-agent-00.html) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The standard is currently in its very first iteration (**version 00**), introduced at the end of February 2025. The most significant "new" development is the formal introduction of **Context-Aware Mapping** specifically for AI workloads. This includes the proposal for the LISP Mapping System to carry non-topological metadata like **Processing Latency (Latency_SLA)** and **Hardware Capability Tags** (e.g., "I need a GPU"). It also introduces the concept of **Dynamic Metadata Updates**, allowing Edge Tunnel Routers (ETRs) to update agent status (load/intent) without the overhead of full de-registration, addressing the high-mobility and high-state-change nature of autonomous AI agents.

**Discussion Summary:**

This standard proposes the use of the Locator/ID Separation Protocol (LISP) as a foundational network layer to facilitate communication between distributed AI agents. As AI systems become increasingly decentralized across cloud, edge, and endpoint environments, they require networking capabilities that traditional IP cannot easily provide, such as location transparency, persistent identity during mobility, and logical isolation. 

The draft, authored by Wei Wang and Chongfeng Xie of China Telecom, argues that LISP's inherent architecture—which decouples an endpoint's identity (EID) from its routing location (RLOC)—naturally addresses these needs. By assigning each AI agent a stable EID, the agent can migrate across different physical hosts or networks without disrupting active communication sessions. Furthermore, LISP's Instance ID (IID) capability allows for the creation of isolated "Agent Groups," ensuring security and multi-tenancy even on shared physical infrastructure.

Key technical contributions of the document include proposed extensions to the LISP framework to make it "context-aware." This involves enhancing the LISP Mapping System to store and retrieve specific AI agent attributes, such as processing latency (Latency_SLA) and hardware capability tags (e.g., GPU availability). These extensions would allow Ingress Tunnel Routers (ITRs) to make intelligent, policy-based routing decisions, ensuring agents are connected to hosts that meet their specific computational or performance requirements. Additionally, the draft suggests enhanced Map-Request/Map-Reply semantics and more efficient metadata update mechanisms to support high-frequency state changes in dynamic AI environments.

---

### VCAP-AP2 Binding: Verified Commerce Settlement for the Agent Payments Protocol (VCAP-AP2 Binding)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/f670b4b7-aa5b-4461-a6f1-84165767f1b3
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-stone-vcap-ap2-binding/
- Tags: Agent Payments Protocol, AP2, VCAP, AI Agents, Escrow, Commerce Settlement, Cryptographic Proofs
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

This specification defines how the Verified Commerce for Agent Protocols (VCAP) settlement layer binds to the Agent Payments Protocol (AP2). It provides the cryptographic proof bindings and verification mechanisms required for escrow fund release based on delivery confirmation in agent-initiated transactions.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Ben Stone (Swarmsync) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/benstone@swarmsync.ai)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)
- Ole Laursen (IOLA) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/OleLaursen)
- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)
- Lars Eggert (IETF @Mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/larseggert)
- Pasi Eronen (reaktor) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pasieronen)
- Ryan Cross — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rpcross)
- Bill Fenner — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fenner)
- Emilio A. (Z1) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/emiliosanchez)

- Adam Roach — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/adamroach)
- Tero Kivinen — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kivinen)
- Kesara Rathnayake (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kesara)
- Matthew Holloway (http://twitter.com/hollowaynz) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holloway)
- Peter Yee — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/Spectre17)
- Jim Fenton (Altmode Networks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimfenton)
- Rich Salz (Akamai Technologies) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/richsalz)
- Eric Vyncke — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/evyncke)
- Suresh Krishnan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sureshkrishnan)
- Jean Mahoney (rfc-editor @ietf) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/ajeanmahoney)
- Joel Halpern (Ericsson) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/JoelHalpern)
- Sangho Na — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/microamp)
- Rudi Matz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rudimatz)
- Mark Donnelly — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/meadmaker)
- Valery Smyslov — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/smyslov)
- Jacobo Tarragón — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jminuscula)
- Jim Schaad — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jimsch)
- Martin Thomson (mozilla) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/martinthomson)
- Vid Luther — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/vidluther)
- Ali (iana-org @icann @iana-internal) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alireza83)
- Tom Pusateri — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/pusateri)
- Shane Kerr — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/shane-kerr)
- supermariofp — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/supermariofp)
- Markus Stenberg — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/fingon)
- bacampbe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bacampbe)
- Harald Alvestrand — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/alvestrand)
- maybe-hello-world (SNL-UCSB) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maybe-hello-world)
- Willem Toorop (NLnet Labs) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wtoorop)
- Tianyi Gao (School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/breakertt)
- sftcd — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sftcd)
- Pete Resnick — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/peteresnick)
- Mat Ford — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/madofo)
- Mark Nottingham — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/mnot)
- Liubov Kurafeeva — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/nectostr)
- John L — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jrlevine)
- Ian Williams (aws) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/aph3rson)
- lushancool — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lushancool)
- Dapeng(Max) Liu (w3ctag) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/maxpassion)
- gnocuil — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/gnocuil)
- Benson Muite — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bkmgit)
- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [IETF Datatracker Page](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-stone-vcap-ap2-binding/) (Primary Spec)
- [Text Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-stone-vcap-ap2-binding-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Version](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-stone-vcap-ap2-binding-00.html) (Documentation)
- [Document History](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-stone-vcap-ap2-binding/history/) (Other)

**What's New:**

The version **-00** draft represents the first formal specification of the VCAP-AP2 binding. It introduces a comprehensive mapping between AP2's `PaymentIntent` and VCAP's `escrow_hold`. Notably, it defines new AP2 webhook events for verification (initiated, completed, and timeout) and provides a cryptographic structure for delivery confirmation that was previously undefined in the core AP2 protocol. It also includes an extensible evidence object to store VCAP-specific verification proofs within AP2 transaction metadata.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **VCAP-AP2 Binding** is an informational Internet-Draft that defines the integration between the **Verified Commerce for Agent Protocols (VCAP)** settlement layer and the **Agent Payments Protocol (AP2)**. While AP2 handles payment intents and mandates for AI agent transactions, it lacks a detailed mechanism for "delivery-confirmation" and escrow release. This specification fills those technical gaps by mapping AP2 fields to VCAP's escrow state machines and cryptographic proof systems.

Key decisions in this binding include the definition of the `authorized` to `captured` state transition based on VCAP verification callbacks. It introduces a new evidence type for AP2—`vcap.verification_proof`—which includes SHA-256 hashes and HMAC signatures to ensure proof integrity. This allows for automated, verifiable settlement where funds are only captured once a delivery is confirmed by a verifier (such as browser automation or LLM evaluation), strictly within a defined dispute window.

Open questions often surround the specific implementations of "verifiers" and the handling of timeouts, which the draft addresses by suggesting manual reviews or escalation to VCAP's internal logic. The standard is designed to ensure that agent-to-agent commerce remains trustless and cryptographically sound across different payment rails.

---

### VCAP: Verified Commerce for Agent Protocols (VCAP)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/8f446234-ae1a-4fbb-964a-799be881e403
- Organization: IETF
- Specification: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-stone-vcap/
- Tags: AI Agents, Autonomous Agents, Payments, Settlement Protocol, Proof of Work Delivery, Cryptographic Verification
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

An open standard for settling financial transactions between autonomous AI agents using cryptographically verifiable proof of work delivery. It defines message formats and state machines for holding funds in escrow and releasing payments based on machine-verifiable evidence.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- Ben Stone (SwarmSyncAI) — Author [profile](https://datatracker.ietf.org/person/benstone@swarmsync.ai)
- Henrik Levkowetz — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/levkowetz)
- Robert Sparks (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/rjsparks)

- Jennifer Richards (ietf-tools) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jennifer-richards)
- Nicolas Giard (ietf @ietf-tools @ietf-llc @requarks) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/NGPixel)

- Warren Kumari — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/wkumari)
- Tim Wicinski — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/moonshiner)
- Spike^ekipS — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/spikeekips)
- Seonghyeon Cho (marqvision) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/sh-cho)
- Richard Barnes — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/bifurcation)
- PriyankaN (Association Management Solutions, LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/PriyankaGitCom)

**Resources & Links:**

- [Primary Specification (Internet-Draft)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-stone-vcap/) (Primary Spec)
- [Text Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-stone-vcap-00.txt) (Documentation)
- [HTML Format](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-stone-vcap-00.html) (Documentation)

**What's New:**

The VCAP specification was recently introduced as an **Internet-Draft (version 00)** in March 2026. This initial release establishes the foundational architecture for AI agent commerce, including:
*   **A Seven-Phase Transaction Flow:** Defining the lifecycle from negotiation and escrow to cryptographic settlement.
*   **Verification Engine Interoperability:** Support for diverse verification methods (LLM-eval, browser automation, etc.) through standardized "Verification Hints."
*   **Cryptographic Bindings:** The introduction of proof hashes and signatures to ensure that settlement is machine-verifiable and auditable.
*   **Marketplace Integration:** Designed to be rail-agnostic, the protocol supports settlement across traditional payment systems (like Stripe), internal wallets, or cryptocurrency.
*   **Compatibility:** Explicit alignment with existing agent standards like Google A2A and the Agent Protocol to act as a specialized settlement layer.

**Discussion Summary:**

Verified Commerce for Agent Protocols (VCAP) is a proposed open standard designed to facilitate secure financial transactions between autonomous AI agents. As AI agents increasingly operate independently—hiring other agents and delegating subtasks—a need has emerged for a vendor-neutral settlement layer that ensures payment is only released upon delivery of verified work. VCAP addresses this by providing a framework of message formats, state machines, and cryptographic bindings that complement existing agent communication protocols like Google A2A or the Agent Protocol.

The core of VCAP is its verification-agnostic approach, allowing for various verification engines—ranging from browser automation and LLM evaluation to human review—to produce compliant proofs. The protocol operates through seven distinct phases: Negotiation, Escrow, Execution, Delivery, Verification, Settlement, and Escalation. This structured approach ensures that funds are held in escrow by a marketplace or platform and are only released to the provider agent once an independent verifier confirms the work meets the agreed-upon criteria.

Technically, VCAP relies on JSON-based message formats for negotiation, escrow holds, and verification callbacks. It emphasizes cryptographic auditability, where every settlement is tied to a proof hash and signature. This allows for independent verification of the transaction history and the evidence of work. Furthermore, the protocol is designed for graceful degradation; if automated verification proves ambiguous or encounters a timeout, the system is designed to route the dispute to human review. Use cases for VCAP include any scenario where machine-to-machine commerce requires trustless settlement, such as automated content generation, data extraction, or specialized API services performed by one AI agent for another.

---

### x402 Extension for the Agent2Agent Protocol (A2A x402)

- Prosecco page: https://prosecco-ai-standards.lovable.app/standard/6127dfb9-dff2-405f-bb01-bbbeee53b247
- Organization: Google
- Specification: https://github.com/google-agentic-commerce/a2a-x402
- Tags: Protocol, Agents, Payments, Transport
- Status: Emerging
- Last updated: 2026-03-23

The A2A x402 Extension enables cryptocurrency payments within the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. It provides a mechanism for agents to monetize services through on-chain payments, reviving the concept of HTTP 402 'Payment Required' for decentralized AI agents.

**Authors & Contributors:**

- google-agentic-commerce (Google) — Author
- Ling (google) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/lingzhong)
- Jordan — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/jorellis)
- Matias A. — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/matiasanaya)
- Holt Skinner (google) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/holtskinner)
- Carson Roscoe — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/CarsonRoscoe)
- Kunal Sekhri (Google LLC) — GitHub Contributor [profile](https://github.com/kunalsekhriG)

**Resources & Links:**

- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/google-agentic-commerce/a2a-x402) (Github)

**What's New:**

Recent activity in the **x402 protocol** has focused on refining the **Automated Dispute Resolution (ADR)** flow. The latest updates include new message types for handling partial refunds and shipping exceptions directly between agents. Additionally, the repository recently added comprehensive **TypeScript type definitions** to improve developer experience for those building agent-compatible storefronts. There has also been a recent shift toward supporting **asynchronous payment confirmations**, allowing for longer-running settlement processes common in B2B commerce.

**Discussion Summary:**

The **x402 Extension for the Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol** is a specialized technical standard designed to facilitate secure and structured commercial transactions between autonomous AI agents. Developed as part of the Google Agentic Commerce initiative, this extension focuses on enhancing the base A2A protocol with specific features required for automated procurement, negotiation, and settlement. 

Key technical aspects include:
- **Commercial Contextualization**: Introduces state machines and message schemas specifically for purchasing, invoicing, and fulfillment.
- **Interoperability**: Ensures that agents from different platforms can exchange standardized commercial intent (e.g., Requests for Quote, Purchase Orders) without proprietary lock-in.
- **Security & Provenance**: Implements cryptographic verification of agent identity and authorization, ensuring that financial commitments made by agents are legally and technically binding.

The standard aims to bridge the gap between large language model (LLM) reasoning and practical, reliable business execution by providing a rigid framework for agent communication in the commerce domain.

---
